Dependent territory under the British Empire; direct rule from the crown

infobox.key: • [Government of India Act](/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1858 "Government of India Act 1858")

infobox.value: "[God Save the King/Queen](/wiki/God_Save_the_Queen "God Save the Queen")" [[1]](#cite_note-1) | |

| **Succeeded by** | | |  |  | | --- | --- | |  | [**1858:** Company rule in India](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India") | |  | [**1857:** Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") | |  | [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire") | | |  |  | | --- | --- | | [Post-British Raj states](/wiki/Legacy_of_the_British_Raj "Legacy of the British Raj") |  | | | |

([/ˈrɑːdʒ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*RAHJ*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[12]](#cite_note-18)[[13]](#cite_note-19) was a transregional [dependental country](/wiki/Dependent_territory "Dependent territory") that spanned much of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and southern [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), existing from 1857 until its [partition](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") in 1947. It was the rule of the [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") on the Indian subcontinent[[14]](#cite_note-def-raj-stacked-20)[[15]](#cite_note-period-of-raj-stacked-21) and thus, it was also called **Crown rule in India**,[[16]](#cite_note-british-raj-crown-rule-stacked-22) **direct rule in India,**[[17]](#cite_note-direct-rule-stacked-23) and **British India.** The region under British control was commonly called **India** in contemporaneous usage and included areas directly administered by the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"), which were collectively called *[British India](/wiki/Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India "Presidencies and provinces of British India")*, and areas ruled by indigenous rulers, but under British [paramountcy](/wiki/British_paramountcy "British paramountcy"), called the [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state"). The region was sometimes called the **Indian Empire** or the **British Indian Empire**, though not officially.[[18]](#cite_note-24) As *India*, it was a founding member of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") and a [founding member](/wiki/Member_states_of_the_United_Nations "Member states of the United Nations") of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") in [San Francisco in 1945](/wiki/United_Nations_Conference_on_International_Organization "United Nations Conference on International Organization").[[19]](#cite_note-mansergh-UN-SanFrancisco2-25) India was a participating state in the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/India_at_the_Olympics "India at the Olympics") in [1900](/wiki/India_at_the_1900_Summer_Olympics "India at the 1900 Summer Olympics"), [1920](/wiki/India_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "India at the 1920 Summer Olympics"), [1928](/wiki/India_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics "India at the 1928 Summer Olympics"), [1932](/wiki/India_at_the_1932_Summer_Olympics "India at the 1932 Summer Olympics"), and [1936](/wiki/India_at_the_1936_Summer_Olympics "India at the 1936 Summer Olympics"). At its time, it was the [second-most populated country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population_in_1900 "List of countries by population in 1900") and the most populated colony within Britain, and also bordered over 5 countries. Its capital was at [Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata") (also the largest city) but later changed to [New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1858–1947 Crown colonial rule in India

This article is about the rule of India by the British Crown from 1858 to 1947. For the rule of the East India Company from 1757 to 1858, see [Company rule in India](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India"). For British directly-ruled administrative divisions in India, see [Presidencies and provinces of British India](/wiki/Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India "Presidencies and provinces of British India").

**British Raj** ([/ˈrɑːdʒ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*RAHJ*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[12]](#cite_note-18)[[13]](#cite_note-19) was a transregional [dependental country](/wiki/Dependent_territory "Dependent territory") that spanned much of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and southern [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), existing from 1857 until its [partition](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") in 1947. It was the rule of the [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") on the Indian subcontinent[[14]](#cite_note-def-raj-stacked-20)[[15]](#cite_note-period-of-raj-stacked-21) and thus, it was also called **Crown rule in India**,[[16]](#cite_note-british-raj-crown-rule-stacked-22) **direct rule in India,**[[17]](#cite_note-direct-rule-stacked-23) and **British India.** The region under British control was commonly called **India** in contemporaneous usage and included areas directly administered by the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"), which were collectively called *[British India](/wiki/Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India "Presidencies and provinces of British India")*, and areas ruled by indigenous rulers, but under British [paramountcy](/wiki/British_paramountcy "British paramountcy"), called the [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state"). The region was sometimes called the **Indian Empire** or the **British Indian Empire**, though not officially.[[18]](#cite_note-24) As *India*, it was a founding member of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") and a [founding member](/wiki/Member_states_of_the_United_Nations "Member states of the United Nations") of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") in [San Francisco in 1945](/wiki/United_Nations_Conference_on_International_Organization "United Nations Conference on International Organization").[[19]](#cite_note-mansergh-UN-SanFrancisco2-25) India was a participating state in the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/India_at_the_Olympics "India at the Olympics") in [1900](/wiki/India_at_the_1900_Summer_Olympics "India at the 1900 Summer Olympics"), [1920](/wiki/India_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "India at the 1920 Summer Olympics"), [1928](/wiki/India_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics "India at the 1928 Summer Olympics"), [1932](/wiki/India_at_the_1932_Summer_Olympics "India at the 1932 Summer Olympics"), and [1936](/wiki/India_at_the_1936_Summer_Olympics "India at the 1936 Summer Olympics"). At its time, it was the [second-most populated country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population_in_1900 "List of countries by population in 1900") and the most populated colony within Britain, and also bordered over 5 countries. Its capital was at [Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata") (also the largest city) but later changed to [New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi").

This system of governance was instituted on 28 June 1858, when, after the [First Indian Revolution](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857"), the [rule](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India") of the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") was transferred to the Crown in the person of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria")[[20]](#cite_note-British_Raj_Independence_movement-26) (who, in 1876, was proclaimed [Empress of India](/wiki/Empress_of_India "Empress of India")). It lasted until 1947 when the British Raj was partitioned into two sovereign [dominion](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") states: the [Dominion of India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India") (later the [Republic of India](/wiki/India "India")) and [Dominion of Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan") (later the [Islamic Republic of Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") and [People's Republic of Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") in the 1971 [Proclamation of Bangladeshi Independence](/wiki/Proclamation_of_Bangladeshi_Independence "Proclamation of Bangladeshi Independence")). At the inception of the Raj in 1858, [Lower Burma](/wiki/Lower_Burma "Lower Burma") was already a part of British India; [Upper Burma](/wiki/Upper_Burma "Upper Burma") was added in 1886, and the resulting union, [Burma](/wiki/British_rule_in_Burma "British rule in Burma"), was administered as an autonomous province until 1937, when it [became a separate British colony](/wiki/Separation_of_British_Burma "Separation of British Burma"), gaining its independence in 1948. It was renamed [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") in 1989. The [Chief Commissioner's Province of Aden](/wiki/Chief_Commissioner%27s_Province_of_Aden "Chief Commissioner's Province of Aden") was also part of the Raj at its inception and became a separate [colony](/wiki/Colony "Colony") known as [Aden Colony](/wiki/Aden_Colony "Aden Colony") in 1937 as well.

## Geographical extent

The British Raj extended over almost all present-day India, Pakistan, Bangladesh and Myanmar, except for small holdings by other European nations such as [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa") (Portugal) and [Pondicherry](/wiki/Puducherry_(city) "Puducherry (city)") (France).[[21]](#cite_note-British_India_geography-27) This area is very diverse, containing the Himalayan mountains, fertile floodplains, the [Indo-Gangetic Plain](/wiki/Indo-Gangetic_Plain "Indo-Gangetic Plain"), a long coastline, tropical dry forests, arid uplands, and the [Thar Desert](/wiki/Thar_Desert "Thar Desert").[[22]](#cite_note-28) In addition, at various times, it included [Aden](/wiki/Aden_Province "Aden Province") (from 1858 to 1937),[[23]](#cite_note-29) [Lower Burma](/wiki/Lower_Burma "Lower Burma") (from 1858 to 1937), [Upper Burma](/wiki/Upper_Burma "Upper Burma") (from 1886 to 1937), [British Somaliland](/wiki/British_Somaliland "British Somaliland") (briefly from 1884 to 1898), and the [Straits Settlements](/wiki/Straits_Settlements "Straits Settlements") (briefly from 1858 to 1867). Burma was [separated from India](/wiki/Separation_of_British_Burma "Separation of British Burma") and directly administered by the British Crown from 1937 until its independence in 1948. To the west, from the early 20th century, the [Trucial States](/wiki/Trucial_States "Trucial States") of the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf") showed signs of more close-knit association with the government of the British Raj,[[24]](#cite_note-igi-v4-trucial-30) which was analogous to that of the princely states.[[25]](#cite_note-KRadford_IHR2013-31)

Among other countries in the region, [Ceylon](/wiki/British_Ceylon "British Ceylon") (now [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka")), which referred to the coastal regions and northern part of the island at that time, was ceded to Britain in 1802 under the [Treaty of Amiens](/wiki/Treaty_of_Amiens "Treaty of Amiens"). These coastal regions were temporarily administered under [Madras Presidency](/wiki/Madras_Presidency "Madras Presidency") between 1793 and 1798,[[26]](#cite_note-codringtonch10-32) but, for later periods, the British governors reported to [London](/wiki/London "London"), and it was not part of the Raj. The kingdoms of [Nepal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Nepal "Kingdom of Nepal") and [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan"), having fought wars with the British, subsequently signed treaties with them and were recognised by the British as independent states.[[27]](#cite_note-33)[[28]](#cite_note-34) The [Kingdom of Sikkim](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sikkim "Kingdom of Sikkim") was established as a princely state after the Anglo-Sikkimese Treaty of 1861; however, the issue of sovereignty was left undefined.[[29]](#cite_note-35) The [Maldive Islands](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives") were a British [protectorate](/wiki/Protectorate "Protectorate") from 1887 to 1965 but not part of British India.[[30]](#cite_note-36)

## History

Main article: [History of the British Raj](/wiki/History_of_the_British_Raj "History of the British Raj")

### 1858–1868: Rebellion aftermath, critiques, and responses

* [![Lakshmibai, Rani of Jhansi, one of the principal leaders of the Great Uprising of 1857, who had lost her kingdom by the Doctrine of lapse](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Rani_of_jhansi.jpg/120px-Rani_of_jhansi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rani_of_jhansi.jpg "Lakshmibai, Rani of Jhansi, one of the principal leaders of the Great Uprising of 1857, who had lost her kingdom by the Doctrine of lapse")

  [Lakshmibai, Rani of Jhansi](/wiki/Rani_Lakshmibai "Rani Lakshmibai"), one of the principal leaders of the [Great Uprising of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857"), who had lost her kingdom by the [Doctrine of lapse](/wiki/Doctrine_of_lapse "Doctrine of lapse")
* [![The proclamation to the "Princes, Chiefs, and People of India", issued by Queen Victoria on 1 November 1858](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Image_victoria_proclamation1858c.JPG/120px-Image_victoria_proclamation1858c.JPG)](/wiki/File:Image_victoria_proclamation1858c.JPG "The proclamation to the \"Princes, Chiefs, and People of India\", issued by Queen Victoria on 1 November 1858")

  The proclamation to the "Princes, Chiefs, and People of India", issued by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") on 1 November 1858
* [![Sir Syed Ahmed Khan founder of the Muhammadan Anglo-Oriental College, wrote one of the early critiques, The Causes of the Indian Mutiny.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/SAKhan.jpg/120px-SAKhan.jpg)](/wiki/File:SAKhan.jpg "Sir Syed Ahmed Khan founder of the Muhammadan Anglo-Oriental College, wrote one of the early critiques, The Causes of the Indian Mutiny.")

  [Sir Syed Ahmed Khan](/wiki/Sir_Syed_Ahmed_Khan "Sir Syed Ahmed Khan") founder of the [Muhammadan Anglo-Oriental College](/wiki/Muhammadan_Anglo-Oriental_College "Muhammadan Anglo-Oriental College"), wrote one of the early critiques, *The Causes of the Indian Mutiny*.
* [![An 1887 souvenir portrait of Queen Victoria as Empress of India, 30 years after the Great Uprising](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Victoria_empress_india1.jpg/120px-Victoria_empress_india1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victoria_empress_india1.jpg "An 1887 souvenir portrait of Queen Victoria as Empress of India, 30 years after the Great Uprising")

  An 1887 souvenir portrait of Queen Victoria as [Empress of India](/wiki/Empress_of_India "Empress of India"), 30 years after the Great Uprising

Although the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857") had shaken the British enterprise in India, it had not derailed it. Until 1857, the British, especially under [Lord Dalhousie](/wiki/James_Broun-Ramsay,_1st_Marquess_of_Dalhousie "James Broun-Ramsay, 1st Marquess of Dalhousie"), had been hurriedly building an India which they envisaged to be on par with Britain itself in the quality and strength of its economic and social institutions. After the rebellion, they became more circumspect. Much thought was devoted to the causes of the rebellion, and three main lessons were drawn. First, at a practical level, it was felt that there needed to be more communication and camaraderie between the British and Indians—not just between British army officers and their Indian staff but in civilian life as well.[[31]](#cite_note-spear147-37) The Indian army was completely reorganised: units composed of the Muslims and Brahmins of the [United Provinces of Agra and Oudh](/wiki/United_Provinces_of_Agra_and_Oudh "United Provinces of Agra and Oudh"), who had formed the core of the rebellion, were disbanded. New regiments, like the Sikhs and Baluchis, composed of Indians who, in British estimation, had demonstrated steadfastness, were formed. From then on, the Indian army was to remain unchanged in its organisation until 1947.[[32]](#cite_note-38) The 1861 Census had revealed that the English population in India was 125,945. Of these, only about 41,862 were civilians as compared with about 84,083 European officers and men of the Army.[[33]](#cite_note-39) In 1880, the standing Indian Army consisted of 66,000 British soldiers, 130,000 Natives, and 350,000 soldiers in the princely armies.[[34]](#cite_note-40)

Second, it was also felt that both the princes and the large land-holders, by not joining the rebellion, had proved to be, in Lord Canning's words, "breakwaters in a storm".[[31]](#cite_note-spear147-37) They too were rewarded in the new British Raj by being integrated into the British-Indian political system and having their territories guaranteed.[[35]](#cite_note-41) At the same time, it was felt that the peasants, for whose benefit the large land reforms of the United Provinces had been undertaken, had shown disloyalty by, in many cases, fighting for their former landlords against the British. Consequently, no more land reforms were implemented for the next 90 years: Bengal and Bihar were to remain the realms of large land holdings (unlike the Punjab and [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh")).[[36]](#cite_note-42)

Third, the British felt disenchanted with Indian reaction to social change. Until the rebellion, they had enthusiastically pushed through social reform, like the ban on *[sati](/wiki/Sati_(practice) "Sati (practice)")* by [Lord William Bentinck](/wiki/Lord_William_Bentinck "Lord William Bentinck").[[37]](#cite_note-43) It was now felt that traditions and customs in India were too strong and too rigid to be changed easily; consequently, no more British social interventions were made, especially in matters dealing with religion,[[38]](#cite_note-spear147-148-44) even when the British felt very strongly about the issue (as in the instance of the remarriage of Hindu child widows).[[39]](#cite_note-45) This sentiment was exemplified further in Queen Victoria's Proclamation released immediately after the rebellion. The proclamation stated that "We disclaim alike our Right and Desire to impose Our Convictions on any of Our Subjects",[[40]](#cite_note-46) demonstrating official British commitment to abstaining from social intervention in India.

### 1858–1880: Railways, canals, Famine Code

* [![Lord Ripon, the Liberal Viceroy of India, who instituted the Famine Code. 1880.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/George_Robinson_1st_Marquess_of_Ripon.jpg/120px-George_Robinson_1st_Marquess_of_Ripon.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Robinson_1st_Marquess_of_Ripon.jpg "Lord Ripon, the Liberal Viceroy of India, who instituted the Famine Code. 1880.")

  [Lord Ripon](/wiki/George_Robinson,_1st_Marquess_of_Ripon "George Robinson, 1st Marquess of Ripon"), the Liberal Viceroy of India, who instituted the Famine Code. 1880.

In the second half of the 19th century, both the direct administration of [India](/wiki/British_India "British India") by the [British crown](/wiki/British_crown "British crown") and the technological change ushered in by the industrial revolution had the effect of closely intertwining the economies of India and Great Britain.[[41]](#cite_note-47) In fact, many of the major changes in transport and communications (that are typically associated with Crown Rule of India) had already begun before the Mutiny. Since Dalhousie had embraced the technological change then rampant in Great Britain, India too saw the rapid development of all those technologies. Railways, roads, canals, and bridges were rapidly built in India, and telegraph links were equally rapidly established so that raw materials, such as cotton, from India's hinterland, could be transported more efficiently to ports, such as [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay"), for subsequent export to England.[[42]](#cite_note-48) Likewise, finished goods from England were transported back for sale in the burgeoning Indian markets.[[43]](#cite_note-oldenburg-49) Unlike Britain, where the market risks for the [infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure "Infrastructure") development were borne by private investors, in India, it was the taxpayers—primarily farmers and farm-labourers—who endured the risks, which, in the end, amounted to £50 million.[[44]](#cite_note-50) Despite these costs, very little skilled employment was created for Indians. By 1920, with the fourth largest railway network in the world and a history of 60 years of its construction, only ten per cent of the "superior posts" in the Indian Railways were held by Indians.[[45]](#cite_note-51)

The rush of technology was also changing the agricultural economy in India: by the last decade of the 19th century, a large fraction of some raw materials—not only cotton but also some food-grains—were being exported to faraway markets.[[46]](#cite_note-stein260-52) Many small farmers, dependent on the whims of those markets, lost land, animals, and equipment to money-lenders.[[46]](#cite_note-stein260-52) The latter half of the 19th century also saw an increase in the number of large-scale [famines in India](/wiki/Famine_in_India "Famine in India"). Although famines were not new to the subcontinent, these were particularly severe, with tens of millions dying[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and with many critics, both British and Indian, laying the blame at the doorsteps of the lumbering colonial administrations.[[46]](#cite_note-stein260-52) There were also salutary effects: commercial cropping, especially in the newly canalled Punjab, led to increased food production for internal consumption.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006126-53) The railway network provided critical famine relief,[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200697-54) notably reduced the cost of moving goods,[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf200697-54) and helped nascent Indian-owned industry.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf2006126-53) After the [Great Famine of 1876–1878](/wiki/Great_Famine_of_1876%E2%80%931878 "Great Famine of 1876–1878"), the Indian Famine Commission report was issued in 1880, and the [Indian Famine Codes](/wiki/Indian_Famine_Codes "Indian Famine Codes"), the earliest [famine scales](/wiki/Famine_scale "Famine scale") and programmes for famine prevention, were instituted.[[49]](#cite_note-55) In one form or other, they would be implemented worldwide by the United Nations and the Food and Agricultural Organisation well into the 1970s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 1880s–1890s: middle class, Indian National Congress

See also: [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress")

* [![Allan Octavian Hume (1829–1912), who proposed the idea of the Indian National Congress in a letter to graduates of Calcutta University](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/A_O_Hume.jpg/120px-A_O_Hume.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_O_Hume.jpg "Allan Octavian Hume (1829–1912), who proposed the idea of the Indian National Congress in a letter to graduates of Calcutta University")

* [![Congress, Bombay, 28 December 1885. Third row (middle) (l. to r.) Dadabhai Naoroji, Hume, W. C. Bonerjee](/wiki/Womesh_Chandra_Bonerjee "Womesh Chandra Bonerjee"), and [Pherozeshah Mehta](/wiki/Pherozeshah_Mehta "Pherozeshah Mehta").
* [![Poverty and the Un-British Rule in India, 1901, by Naoroji, Member, British Parliament (1892–1895), and Congress president (1886, 1893, 1906)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Poverty_and_Un_British_Rule_in_India1.jpg/120px-Poverty_and_Un_British_Rule_in_India1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Poverty_and_Un_British_Rule_in_India1.jpg "Poverty and the Un-British Rule in India, 1901, by Naoroji, Member, British Parliament (1892–1895), and Congress president (1886, 1893, 1906)")

  *Poverty and the Un-British Rule in India*, 1901, by Naoroji, Member, British Parliament (1892–1895), and Congress president (1886, 1893, 1906)
* [![Mehta, lawyer, businessman, and president of the sixth session of the Indian National Congress in 1890](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/52/Pherozeshah_Mehta_%28portrait%29.jpg/120px-Pherozeshah_Mehta_%28portrait%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pherozeshah_Mehta_(portrait).jpg "Mehta, lawyer, businessman, and president of the sixth session of the Indian National Congress in 1890")

  [Mehta](/wiki/Pherozeshah_Mehta "Pherozeshah Mehta"), lawyer, businessman, and president of the sixth session of the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") in 1890

By 1880, a new middle class had arisen in India and spread thinly across the country. Moreover, there was a growing solidarity among its members, created by the "joint stimuli of encouragement and irritation".[[50]](#cite_note-56) The encouragement felt by this class came from its success in education and its ability to avail itself of the benefits of that education such as employment in the [Indian Civil Service](/wiki/Indian_Civil_Service "Indian Civil Service"). It came too from Queen Victoria's proclamation of 1858 in which she had declared, "We hold ourselves bound to the natives of our Indian territories by the same obligation of duty which bind us to all our other subjects."[[51]](#cite_note-majumdar888-57) Indians were especially encouraged when Canada was granted [dominion status](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") in 1867 and established an autonomous democratic constitution.[[51]](#cite_note-majumdar888-57) Lastly, the encouragement came from the work of contemporaneous Oriental scholars like [Monier Monier-Williams](/wiki/Monier_Monier-Williams "Monier Monier-Williams") and [Max Müller](/wiki/Max_M%C3%BCller "Max Müller"), who in their works had been presenting ancient India as a great civilisation. Irritation, on the other hand, came not just from incidents of racial discrimination at the hands of the British in India but also from governmental actions like the use of Indian troops in imperial campaigns (e.g., in the [Second Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Afghan_War "Second Anglo-Afghan War")) and the attempts to control the vernacular press (e.g., in the *[Vernacular Press Act](/wiki/Vernacular_Press_Act "Vernacular Press Act") of 1878*).[[52]](#cite_note-58)

It was, however, Viceroy [Lord Ripon](/wiki/Lord_Ripon "Lord Ripon")'s partial reversal of the [Ilbert Bill](/wiki/Ilbert_Bill "Ilbert Bill") (1883), a legislative measure that had proposed putting Indian judges in the [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency") on equal footing with British ones, that transformed the discontent into political action.[[53]](#cite_note-spear170-59) On 28 December 1885, professionals and intellectuals from this middle-class—many educated at the new British-founded universities in Bombay, Calcutta, and Madras, and familiar with the ideas of British political philosophers, especially the [utilitarians](/wiki/Utilitarianism#Classical_utilitarianism "Utilitarianism") assembled in Bombay—founded the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress"). The 70 men elected [Womesh Chunder Bonerjee](/wiki/Womesh_Chunder_Bonnerjee "Womesh Chunder Bonnerjee") as the first president. The membership consisted of a westernised elite, and no effort was made at this time to broaden the base.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During its first 20 years, the Congress primarily debated British policy toward India. Its debates created a new Indian outlook that held Great Britain responsible for draining India of its wealth. Britain did this, the nationalists claimed, by unfair trade, by the restraint on indigenous Indian industry, and by the use of Indian taxes to pay the high salaries of the British civil servants in India.[[54]](#cite_note-60)

[Thomas Baring](/wiki/Thomas_Baring,_1st_Earl_of_Northbrook "Thomas Baring, 1st Earl of Northbrook") served as Viceroy of India 1872–1876. Baring's major accomplishments came as an energetic reformer who was dedicated to upgrading the quality of government in the British Raj. He began large scale famine relief, reduced taxes, and overcame bureaucratic obstacles in an effort to reduce both starvation and widespread social unrest. Although appointed by a Liberal government, his policies were much the same as viceroys appointed by Conservative governments.[[55]](#cite_note-61)

Social reform was in the air by the 1880s. For example, [Pandita Ramabai](/wiki/Pandita_Ramabai "Pandita Ramabai"), poet, Sanskrit scholar, and a champion of the emancipation of Indian women, took up the cause of widow remarriage, especially of Brahmin widows, later converted to Christianity.[[56]](#cite_note-62) By 1900, reform movements had taken root within the Indian National Congress. Congress member [Gopal Krishna Gokhale](/wiki/Gopal_Krishna_Gokhale "Gopal Krishna Gokhale") founded the [Servants of India Society](/wiki/Servants_of_India_Society "Servants of India Society"), which lobbied for legislative reform (e.g., for a law to permit the remarriage of Hindu child widows) and whose members took vows of poverty, and worked among the [untouchable community](/wiki/Dalits "Dalits").[[57]](#cite_note-63)

By 1905, a deep gulf opened between the moderates, led by Gokhale, who downplayed public agitation, and the new "extremists" who not only advocated agitation but also regarded the pursuit of social reform as a distraction from nationalism. Prominent among the extremists was [Bal Gangadhar Tilak](/wiki/Bal_Gangadhar_Tilak "Bal Gangadhar Tilak"), who attempted to mobilise Indians by appealing to an explicitly Hindu political identity, displayed, for example, in the annual public [Ganapati](/wiki/Ganapati "Ganapati") festivals that he inaugurated in western India.[[58]](#cite_note-64)

### 1870s–1906: Muslim social movements, Muslim League

* [![Lord Minto, the viceroy who replaced Curzon in 1906. The Minto-Morley Reforms of 1909 allowed separate Muslim electorates.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/LordMelgund1885.jpg/120px-LordMelgund1885.jpg)](/wiki/File:LordMelgund1885.jpg "Lord Minto, the viceroy who replaced Curzon in 1906. The Minto-Morley Reforms of 1909 allowed separate Muslim electorates.")

  [Lord Minto](/wiki/Gilbert_John_Elliot-Murray-Kynynmound,_4th_Earl_of_Minto "Gilbert John Elliot-Murray-Kynynmound, 4th Earl of Minto"), the viceroy who replaced Curzon in 1906. The [Minto-Morley Reforms](/wiki/Minto-Morley_Reforms "Minto-Morley Reforms") of 1909 allowed separate Muslim electorates.
* [![1909 Prevailing Religions, map of the British Indian Empire, 1909, showing the majority religions based on the Census of 1901](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Brit_IndianEmpireReligions3.jpg/120px-Brit_IndianEmpireReligions3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brit_IndianEmpireReligions3.jpg "1909 Prevailing Religions, map of the British Indian Empire, 1909, showing the majority religions based on the Census of 1901")

  *1909 Prevailing Religions*, map of the British Indian Empire, 1909, showing the majority religions based on the Census of 1901
* [![Hakim Ajmal Khan, a founder of the Muslim League, was to also become the president of the Indian National Congress in 1921.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/1921ajmalkhan.jpg/120px-1921ajmalkhan.jpg)](/wiki/File:1921ajmalkhan.jpg "Hakim Ajmal Khan, a founder of the Muslim League, was to also become the president of the Indian National Congress in 1921.")

  [Hakim Ajmal Khan](/wiki/Hakim_Ajmal_Khan "Hakim Ajmal Khan"), a founder of the Muslim League, was to also become the president of the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") in 1921.

The overwhelming, but predominantly Hindu, protest against the partition of Bengal and the fear in its wake of reforms favouring the Hindu majority, led the Muslim elite in India to meet with the new viceroy, [Lord Minto](/wiki/Lord_Minto "Lord Minto")
in 1906 and to ask for separate electorates for Muslims.[[43]](#cite_note-oldenburg-49) In conjunction, they demanded proportional legislative representation reflecting both their status as former rulers and their record of cooperating with the British. This led,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] in December 1906, to the founding of the [All-India Muslim League](/wiki/All-India_Muslim_League "All-India Muslim League") in [Dacca](/wiki/Dacca "Dacca"). Although Curzon, by now, had resigned his position over a dispute with his military chief [Lord Kitchener](/wiki/Herbert_Kitchener,_1st_Earl_Kitchener "Herbert Kitchener, 1st Earl Kitchener") and returned to England, the League was in favour of his partition plan.[[59]](#cite_note-ludden200-65) The Muslim elite's position, which was reflected in the League's position, had crystallized gradually over the previous three decades, beginning with the revelations of the Census of British India in 1871,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] which had for the first time estimated the populations in regions of the Muslim majority[[59]](#cite_note-ludden200-65) (for his part, Curzon's desire to court the Muslims of East Bengal had arisen from British anxieties ever since the 1871 census—and in light of the history of Muslims fighting them in the [1857 Mutiny](/wiki/Indian_Mutiny_of_1857 "Indian Mutiny of 1857") and the [Second Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Afghan_War "Second Anglo-Afghan War")—about Indian Muslims rebelling against the Crown).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the three decades since, Muslim leaders across northern India had intermittently experienced public animosity from some of the new Hindu political and social groups.[[59]](#cite_note-ludden200-65) The [Arya Samaj](/wiki/Arya_Samaj "Arya Samaj"), for example, had not only supported Cow Protection Societies in their agitation,[[60]](#cite_note-66) but also—distraught at the 1871 Census's Muslim numbers—organized "reconversion" events for the purpose of welcoming Muslims back to the Hindu fold.[[59]](#cite_note-ludden200-65) In 1905, when Tilak and Lajpat Rai attempted to rise to leadership positions in the Congress, and the Congress itself rallied around the symbolism of Kali, Muslim fears increased.[[61]](#cite_note-ludden201-67) It was not lost on many Muslims, for example, that the rallying cry, "Bande Mataram", had first appeared in the novel *[Anand Math](/wiki/Anand_Math "Anand Math")* in which Hindus had battled their Muslim oppressors.[[61]](#cite_note-ludden201-67) Lastly, the Muslim elite, and among it [Dacca Nawab](/wiki/Dhaka_Nawab_Family "Dhaka Nawab Family"), [Khwaja Salimullah](/wiki/Khwaja_Salimullah "Khwaja Salimullah"), who hosted the League's first meeting in his mansion in [Shahbag](/wiki/Shahbag "Shahbag"), was aware that a new province with a Muslim majority would directly benefit Muslims aspiring to political power.[[61]](#cite_note-ludden201-67)

The first steps were taken toward self-government in British India in the late 19th century with the appointment of Indian counsellors to advise the British viceroy and the establishment of provincial councils with Indian members; the British subsequently widened participation in legislative councils with the [Indian Councils Act 1892](/wiki/Indian_Councils_Act_1892 "Indian Councils Act 1892"). [Municipal Corporations](/wiki/Municipal_Corporation "Municipal Corporation") and District Boards were created for local administration; they included elected Indian members.

The [Indian Councils Act 1909](/wiki/Indian_Councils_Act_1909 "Indian Councils Act 1909"), known as the Morley-Minto Reforms ([John Morley](/wiki/John_Morley "John Morley") was the secretary of state for India, and [Minto](/wiki/Gilbert_John_Elliot-Murray-Kynynmound,_4th_Earl_of_Minto "Gilbert John Elliot-Murray-Kynynmound, 4th Earl of Minto") was viceroy)—gave Indians limited roles in the central and provincial legislatures. Upper-class Indians, rich landowners and businessmen were favoured. The Muslim community was made a separate electorate and granted double representation. The goals were quite conservative but they did advance the elective principle.[[62]](#cite_note-Das1964-68)

The partition of Bengal was rescinded in 1911 and announced at the Delhi Durbar at which King [George V](/wiki/George_V_of_the_United_Kingdom "George V of the United Kingdom") came in person and was crowned [Emperor of India](/wiki/Emperor_of_India "Emperor of India"). He announced the capital would be moved from Calcutta to Delhi. This period saw an increase in the activities of [revolutionary groups](/wiki/Revolutionary_movement_for_Indian_independence "Revolutionary movement for Indian independence"), which included Bengal's [Anushilan Samiti](/wiki/Anushilan_Samiti "Anushilan Samiti") and the Punjab's [Ghadar Party](/wiki/Ghadar_Party "Ghadar Party"). However, the British authorities were able to crush violent rebels swiftly, partly because the mainstream of educated Indian politicians opposed violent revolution.[[63]](#cite_note-69)

### 1905–1911: Partition of Bengal, *Swadeshi*, violence

Main articles: [Partition of Bengal (1905)](/wiki/Partition_of_Bengal_(1905) "Partition of Bengal (1905)") and [Swadeshi movement](/wiki/Swadeshi_movement "Swadeshi movement")

* [![Lord Curzon, Viceroy of India, 1899–1905, who partitioned the Bengal Presidency in 1905](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/George_Curzon2.jpg/120px-George_Curzon2.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Curzon2.jpg "Lord Curzon, Viceroy of India, 1899–1905, who partitioned the Bengal Presidency in 1905")

  [Lord Curzon](/wiki/George_Nathaniel_Curzon "George Nathaniel Curzon"), [Viceroy of India](/wiki/Viceroy_of_India "Viceroy of India"), 1899–1905, who partitioned the [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency") in 1905
* [![Congress moderate Sir Surendranath Banerjee led the opposition with the Swadeshi movement.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Surendranath_Banerjee.jpg/120px-Surendranath_Banerjee.jpg)](/wiki/File:Surendranath_Banerjee.jpg "Congress moderate Sir Surendranath Banerjee led the opposition with the Swadeshi movement.")

  Congress moderate Sir Surendranath Banerjee led the opposition with the *Swadeshi* movement.
* [![Tamil magazine, Vijaya, 1909, showing "Mother India" with her progeny and the slogan "Vande Mataram"](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/1909magazine_vijaya.jpg/120px-1909magazine_vijaya.jpg)](/wiki/File:1909magazine_vijaya.jpg "Tamil magazine, Vijaya, 1909, showing \"Mother India\" with her progeny and the slogan \"Vande Mataram\"")

  Tamil magazine, *Vijaya*, 1909, showing "Mother India" with her progeny and the slogan "Vande Mataram"

The viceroy, [Lord Curzon](/wiki/George_Curzon,_1st_Marquess_Curzon_of_Kedleston "George Curzon, 1st Marquess Curzon of Kedleston") (1899–1905), was unusually energetic in pursuit of efficiency and reform.[[64]](#cite_note-70) His agenda included the creation of the [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province_(1901%E2%80%931955) "North-West Frontier Province (1901–1955)"); small changes in the civil services; speeding up the operations of the secretariat; setting up a gold standard to ensure a stable currency; creation of a Railway Board; irrigation reform; reduction of peasant debts; lowering the cost of telegrams; archaeological research and the preservation of antiquities; improvements in the universities; police reforms; upgrading the roles of the Native States; a new Commerce and Industry Department; promotion of industry; revised land revenue policies; lowering taxes; setting up agricultural banks; creating an Agricultural Department; sponsoring agricultural research; establishing an Imperial Library; creating an Imperial Cadet Corps; new famine codes; and, indeed, reducing the smoke nuisance in Calcutta.[[65]](#cite_note-71)

Trouble emerged for Curzon when he divided the largest administrative subdivision in British India, the [Bengal Province](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency"), into the Muslim-majority province of [Eastern Bengal and Assam](/wiki/Eastern_Bengal_and_Assam "Eastern Bengal and Assam") and the Hindu-majority province of West Bengal (present-day Indian states of [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal"), [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar"), and [Odisha](/wiki/Odisha "Odisha")). Curzon's act, the [Partition of Bengal](/wiki/Partition_of_Bengal_(1905) "Partition of Bengal (1905)"), had been contemplated by various colonial administrations since the time of Lord William Bentinck but was never acted upon. Though some considered it administratively felicitous, it was communally charged. It sowed the seeds of division among Indians in Bengal, transforming nationalist politics as nothing else before it. The Hindu elite of Bengal, among them many who owned land in East Bengal that was leased out to Muslim peasants, protested fervidly.[[66]](#cite_note-72)

Following the [Partition of Bengal](/wiki/Partition_of_Bengal_(1905) "Partition of Bengal (1905)"), which was a strategy set out by Lord Curzon to weaken the nationalist movement, Tilak encouraged the [Swadeshi movement](/wiki/Swadeshi_movement "Swadeshi movement") and the Boycott movement.[[67]](#cite_note-73) The movement consisted of the boycott of foreign goods and also the social boycott of any Indian who used foreign goods. The Swadeshi movement consisted of the usage of natively produced goods. Once foreign goods were boycotted, there was a gap which had to be filled by the production of those goods in India itself. Bal Gangadhar Tilak said that the Swadeshi and Boycott movements are two sides of the same coin. The large Bengali Hindu middle-class (the *[Bhadralok](/wiki/Bhadralok "Bhadralok")*), upset at the prospect of Bengalis being outnumbered in the new Bengal province by Biharis and Oriyas, felt that Curzon's act was punishment for their political assertiveness. The pervasive protests against Curzon's decision took the form predominantly of the *[Swadeshi](/wiki/Swadeshi "Swadeshi")* ("buy Indian") campaign led by two-time Congress president, [Surendranath Banerjee](/wiki/Surendranath_Banerjee "Surendranath Banerjee"), and involved boycott of British goods.[[68]](#cite_note-74)

The rallying cry for both types of protest was the slogan *[Bande Mataram](/wiki/Bande_Mataram "Bande Mataram")* ("Hail to the Mother"), which invoked a mother goddess, who stood variously for Bengal, India, and the Hindu goddess [Kali](/wiki/Kali "Kali"). [Sri Aurobindo](/wiki/Sri_Aurobindo "Sri Aurobindo") never went beyond the law when he edited the *[Bande Mataram](/wiki/Bande_Mataram_(publication) "Bande Mataram (publication)")* magazine; it preached independence but within the bounds of peace as far as possible. Its goal was Passive Resistance.[[69]](#cite_note-75) The unrest spread from Calcutta to the surrounding regions of Bengal when students returned home to their villages and towns. Some joined [local political youth clubs](/wiki/Anushilan_samiti "Anushilan samiti") emerging in Bengal at the time, some engaged in robberies to fund arms, and even attempted to take the lives of Raj officials. However, the conspiracies generally failed in the face of intense police work.[[70]](#cite_note-bandyo260-76) The *Swadeshi* boycott movement cut imports of British textiles by 25%. The *swadeshi* cloth, although more expensive and somewhat less comfortable than its Lancashire competitor, was worn as a mark of national pride by people all over India.[[71]](#cite_note-77)

### 1914–1918: First World War, Lucknow Pact, Home Rule leagues

See also: [Indian Army during World War I](/wiki/Indian_Army_during_World_War_I "Indian Army during World War I") and [Lucknow Pact](/wiki/Lucknow_Pact "Lucknow Pact")

  [Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah"), seated, third from the left, supported the Lucknow Pact in 1916, ending the Muslim League-Congress rift.

The [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") would prove to be a watershed in the imperial relationship between Britain and India. Shortly before the outbreak of war, the Government of India had indicated that they could furnish two divisions plus a cavalry brigade, with a further division in case of emergency.[[72]](#cite_note-India's_contribution-79) Some 1.4 million Indian and British soldiers of the [British Indian Army](/wiki/British_Indian_Army "British Indian Army") took part in the war, primarily in Iraq and the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_theatre_of_World_War_I "Middle Eastern theatre of World War I"). Their participation had a wider cultural fallout as news spread of how bravely soldiers fought and died alongside British soldiers, as well as soldiers from dominions like Canada and Australia.[[73]](#cite_note-brown-p197-198-80) India's international profile rose during the 1920s, as it became a [founding member](/wiki/League_of_Nations_members#1920:_founder_members "League of Nations members") of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") in 1920 and participated, under the name "Les Indes Anglaises" (British India), in the [1920 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics") in Antwerp.[[74]](#cite_note-81) Back in India, especially among the leaders of the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress"), the war led to calls for greater self-government for Indians.[[73]](#cite_note-brown-p197-198-80)

At the onset of World War I, the reassignment of most of the British army in India to Europe and [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamian_campaign "Mesopotamian campaign"), had led the previous viceroy, [Lord Harding](/wiki/Charles_Hardinge,_1st_Baron_Hardinge_of_Penshurst "Charles Hardinge, 1st Baron Hardinge of Penshurst"), to worry about the "risks involved in denuding India of troops".[[73]](#cite_note-brown-p197-198-80) [Revolutionary violence](/wiki/Revolutionary_movement_for_Indian_independence "Revolutionary movement for Indian independence") had already been a concern in British India; consequently, in 1915, to strengthen its powers during what it saw was a time of increased vulnerability, the Government of India passed the [Defence of India Act 1915](/wiki/Defence_of_India_Act_1915 "Defence of India Act 1915"), which allowed it to intern politically dangerous dissidents without due process, and added to the power it already had under the [Indian Press Act, 1910](/wiki/Indian_Press_Act,_1910 "Indian Press Act, 1910") to imprison journalists without trial and to censor the press.[[75]](#cite_note-brown-p201-202-82) It was under the Defence of India act that the Ali brothers were imprisoned in 1916, and [Annie Besant](/wiki/Annie_Besant "Annie Besant"), a European woman, and ordinarily more problematic to imprison, was arrested in 1917.[[75]](#cite_note-brown-p201-202-82) Now, as constitutional reform began to be discussed in earnest, the British began to consider how new moderate Indians could be brought into the fold of constitutional politics and, simultaneously, how the hand of established constitutionalists could be strengthened. However, since the Government of India wanted to ensure against any sabotage of the reform process by extremists, and since its reform plan was devised during a time when extremist violence had ebbed as a result of increased governmental control, it also began to consider how some of its wartime powers could be extended into peacetime.[[75]](#cite_note-brown-p201-202-82)

After the 1906 split between the moderates and the extremists in the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress"), organised political activity by the Congress had remained fragmented until 1914, when [Bal Gangadhar Tilak](/wiki/Bal_Gangadhar_Tilak "Bal Gangadhar Tilak") was released from prison and began to sound out other Congress leaders about possible reunification. That, however, had to wait until the demise of Tilak's principal moderate opponents, [Gopal Krishna Gokhale](/wiki/Gopal_Krishna_Gokhale "Gopal Krishna Gokhale") and [Pherozeshah Mehta](/wiki/Pherozeshah_Mehta "Pherozeshah Mehta"), in 1915, whereupon an agreement was reached for Tilak's ousted group to re-enter the Congress.[[73]](#cite_note-brown-p197-198-80) In the 1916 Lucknow session of the Congress, Tilak's supporters were able to push through a more radical resolution which asked for the British to declare that it was their "aim and intention ... to confer self-government on India at an early date".[[73]](#cite_note-brown-p197-198-80) Soon, other such rumblings began to appear in public pronouncements: in 1917, in the [Imperial Legislative Council](/wiki/Imperial_Legislative_Council "Imperial Legislative Council"), [Madan Mohan Malaviya](/wiki/Madan_Mohan_Malaviya "Madan Mohan Malaviya") spoke of the expectations the war had generated in India, "I venture to say that the war has put the clock ... fifty years forward ... (The) reforms after the war will have to be such, ... as will satisfy the aspirations of her (India's) people to take their legitimate part in the administration of their own country."[[73]](#cite_note-brown-p197-198-80)

The 1916 Lucknow Session of the Congress was also the venue of an unanticipated mutual effort by the Congress and the Muslim League, the occasion for which was provided by the wartime partnership between Germany and Turkey. Since the [Turkish Sultan](/wiki/Ottoman_Caliphate "Ottoman Caliphate"), or *Khalifah*, had also sporadically claimed guardianship of the Islamic holy sites of [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca"), [Medina](/wiki/Medina "Medina"), and [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), and since the British and their allies were now in conflict with Turkey, doubts began to increase among some Indian Muslims about the "religious neutrality" of the British, doubts that had already surfaced as a result of the [reunification of Bengal](/wiki/Partition_of_Bengal_(1905) "Partition of Bengal (1905)") in 1911, a decision that was seen as ill-disposed to Muslims.[[76]](#cite_note-brown-p200-201-83) In the [Lucknow Pact](/wiki/Lucknow_Pact "Lucknow Pact"), the League joined the Congress in the proposal for greater self-government that was campaigned for by Tilak and his supporters; in return, the Congress accepted separate electorates for Muslims in the provincial legislatures as well as the Imperial Legislative Council. In 1916, the Muslim League had anywhere between 500 and 800 members and did not yet have the wider following among Indian Muslims that it enjoyed in later years; in the League itself, the pact did not have unanimous backing, having largely been negotiated by a group of "Young Party" Muslims from the [United Provinces](/wiki/United_Provinces_of_Agra_and_Oudh "United Provinces of Agra and Oudh") (UP), most prominently, two brothers [Mohammad](/wiki/Maulana_Mohammad_Ali "Maulana Mohammad Ali") and [Shaukat Ali](/wiki/Maulana_Shaukat_Ali "Maulana Shaukat Ali"), who had embraced the Pan-Islamic cause;[[76]](#cite_note-brown-p200-201-83) however, it did have the support of a young lawyer from Bombay, [Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah"), who was later to rise to leadership roles in both the League and the Indian independence movement. In later years, as the full ramifications of the pact unfolded, it was seen as benefiting the Muslim minority *élites* of provinces like UP and Bihar more than the Muslim majorities of Punjab and Bengal; nonetheless, at the time, the "Lucknow Pact" was an important milestone in nationalistic agitation and was seen as such by the British.[[76]](#cite_note-brown-p200-201-83)

During 1916, two [Home Rule Leagues](/wiki/Home_Rule_Movement "Home Rule Movement") were founded within the Indian National Congress by Tilak and [Annie Besant](/wiki/Annie_Besant "Annie Besant"), respectively, to promote [Home Rule](/wiki/Home_Rule "Home Rule") among Indians, and also to elevate the stature of the founders within the Congress itself.[[77]](#cite_note-brown-p199-84) Besant, for her part, was also keen to demonstrate the superiority of this new form of organised agitation, which had achieved some success in the [Irish home rule movement](/wiki/Home_Rule#Irish_home_rule "Home Rule"), over the political violence that had intermittently plagued the subcontinent during the years 1907–1914.[[77]](#cite_note-brown-p199-84) The two Leagues focused their attention on complementary geographical regions: Tilak's in western India, in the [southern Bombay presidency](/wiki/Bombay_presidency#Geography "Bombay presidency"), and Besant's in the rest of the country, but especially in the [Madras Presidency](/wiki/Madras_Presidency "Madras Presidency") and in regions like [Sind](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh") and [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") that had hitherto been considered politically dormant by the Congress.[[77]](#cite_note-brown-p199-84) Both leagues rapidly acquired new members—approximately thirty thousand each in a little over a year—and began to publish inexpensive newspapers. Their propaganda also turned to posters, pamphlets, and political-religious songs, and later to mass meetings, which not only attracted greater numbers than in earlier Congress sessions, but also entirely new social groups such as non-[Brahmins](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin"), traders, farmers, students, and lower-level government workers.[[77]](#cite_note-brown-p199-84) Although they did not achieve the magnitude or character of a nationwide mass movement, the Home Rule leagues both deepened and widened organised political agitation for self-rule in India. The British authorities reacted by imposing restrictions on the Leagues, including shutting out students from meetings and banning the two leaders from travelling to certain provinces.[[77]](#cite_note-brown-p199-84)

### 1915–1918: return of Gandhi

See also: [Champaran Satyagraha](/wiki/Champaran_Satyagraha "Champaran Satyagraha") and [Kheda Satyagraha of 1918](/wiki/Kheda_Satyagraha_of_1918 "Kheda Satyagraha of 1918")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Gandhi_back_in_india1915.gif/250px-Gandhi_back_in_india1915.gif)](/wiki/File:Gandhi_back_in_india1915.gif)

[Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi") (seated in carriage, on the right, eyes downcast, with black flat-top hat) receiving a big welcome in [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi") in 1916 after his return to India from [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Gandhi_Kheda_1918.jpg/250px-Gandhi_Kheda_1918.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gandhi_Kheda_1918.jpg)

Gandhi at the time of the Kheda Satyagraha, 1918

The year 1915 also saw the return of [Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi](/wiki/Mohandas_Karamchand_Gandhi "Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi") to India. Already known in India as a result of his civil liberties protests on behalf of the Indians in South Africa, Gandhi followed the advice of his mentor [Gopal Krishna Gokhale](/wiki/Gopal_Krishna_Gokhale "Gopal Krishna Gokhale") and chose not to make any public pronouncements during the first year of his return, but instead spent the year travelling, observing the country at first hand, and writing.[[78]](#cite_note-brown-p214-215-85) Earlier, during his South Africa sojourn, Gandhi, a lawyer by profession, had represented an Indian community, which, although small, was sufficiently diverse to be a microcosm of India itself. In tackling the challenge of holding this community together and simultaneously confronting the colonial authority, he had created a technique of non-violent resistance, which he labelled *[Satyagraha](/wiki/Satyagraha "Satyagraha")* (or Striving for Truth).[[79]](#cite_note-brown-p210-213-86) For Gandhi, *Satyagraha* was different from "[passive resistance](/wiki/Passive_resistance "Passive resistance")", by then a familiar technique of social protest, which he regarded as a practical strategy adopted by the weak in the face of superior force; *Satyagraha*, on the other hand, was for him the "last resort of those strong enough in their commitment to truth to undergo suffering in its cause".[[79]](#cite_note-brown-p210-213-86) [Ahimsa](/wiki/Ahimsa "Ahimsa") or "non-violence", which formed the underpinning of *Satyagraha*, came to represent the twin pillar, with Truth, of Gandhi's unorthodox religious outlook on life.[[79]](#cite_note-brown-p210-213-86) During the years 1907–1914, Gandhi tested the technique of *Satyagraha* in a number of protests on behalf of the Indian community in South Africa against the unjust racial laws.[[79]](#cite_note-brown-p210-213-86)

Also, during his time in South Africa, in his essay, *Hind Swaraj*, (1909), Gandhi formulated his vision of *[Swaraj](/wiki/Swaraj "Swaraj")*, or "self-rule" for India based on three vital ingredients: solidarity between Indians of different faiths, but most of all between Hindus and Muslims; the removal of [untouchability](/wiki/Untouchability "Untouchability") from Indian society; and the exercise of *[swadeshi](/wiki/Swadeshi "Swadeshi")*—the boycott of manufactured foreign goods and the revival of Indian [cottage industry](/wiki/Cottage_industry "Cottage industry").[[78]](#cite_note-brown-p214-215-85) The first two, he felt, were essential for India to be an egalitarian and tolerant society, one befitting the principles of Truth and *[Ahimsa](/wiki/Ahimsa "Ahimsa")*, while the last, by making Indians more self-reliant, would break the cycle of dependence that was perpetuating not only the direction and tenor of the British rule in India, but also the British commitment to it.[[78]](#cite_note-brown-p214-215-85) At least until 1920, the British presence itself was not a stumbling block in Gandhi's conception of *[swaraj](/wiki/Swaraj "Swaraj")*; rather, it was the inability of Indians to create a modern society.[[78]](#cite_note-brown-p214-215-85)

Gandhi made his political debut in India in 1917 in [Champaran](/wiki/Champaran "Champaran") district in [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar"), near the Nepal border, where he was invited by a group of disgruntled tenant farmers who, for many years, had been forced into planting [indigo](/wiki/Indigo "Indigo") (for dyes) on a portion of their land and then selling it at below-market prices to the British planters who had leased them the land.[[80]](#cite_note-brown-p216-217-87) Upon his arrival in the district, Gandhi was joined by other agitators, including a young Congress leader, [Rajendra Prasad](/wiki/Rajendra_Prasad "Rajendra Prasad"), from Bihar, who would become a loyal supporter of Gandhi and go on to play a prominent role in the Indian independence movement. When Gandhi was ordered to leave by the local British authorities, he refused on moral grounds, setting up his refusal as a form of individual [Satyagraha](/wiki/Satyagraha "Satyagraha"). Soon, under pressure from the Viceroy in Delhi who was anxious to maintain domestic peace during wartime, the provincial government rescinded Gandhi's expulsion order, and later agreed to an official enquiry into the case. Although the British planters eventually gave in, they were not won over to the farmers' cause, and thereby did not produce the optimal outcome of a Satyagraha that Gandhi had hoped for; similarly, the farmers themselves, although pleased at the resolution, responded less than enthusiastically to the concurrent projects of rural empowerment and education that Gandhi had inaugurated in keeping with his ideal of *swaraj*. The following year Gandhi launched two more Satyagrahas—both in his native [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat")—one in the rural [Kaira](/wiki/Kheda "Kheda") district where land-owning farmers were protesting increased land-revenue and the other in the city of [Ahmedabad](/wiki/Ahmedabad "Ahmedabad"), where workers in an Indian-owned textile mill were distressed about their low wages. The satyagraha in Ahmedabad took the form of Gandhi fasting and supporting the workers in a strike, which eventually led to a settlement. In Kaira, in contrast, although the farmers' cause received publicity from Gandhi's presence, the satyagraha itself, which consisted of the farmers' collective decision to withhold payment, was not immediately successful, as the British authorities refused to back down. The agitation in Kaira gained for Gandhi another lifelong lieutenant in [Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel](/wiki/Sardar_Vallabhbhai_Patel "Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel"), who had organised the farmers, and who too would go on to play a leadership role in the Indian independence movement.[[81]](#cite_note-88)

### 1916–1919: Montagu–Chelmsford reforms

See also: [Montagu–Chelmsford Reforms](/wiki/Montagu%E2%80%93Chelmsford_Reforms "Montagu–Chelmsford Reforms")

[![Montagu](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Edwin_Samuel_Montagu.jpg/250px-Edwin_Samuel_Montagu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edwin_Samuel_Montagu.jpg)

[Edwin Montagu](/wiki/Edwin_Montagu "Edwin Montagu"), the secretary of state for India, whose report led to the [Government of India Act 1919](/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1919 "Government of India Act 1919"), also known as the Montford Reforms or the Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms

[![Chelmsford](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Chelmsford_Governor.jpg/250px-Chelmsford_Governor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chelmsford_Governor.jpg)

[Lord Chelmsford](/wiki/Frederic_Thesiger,_1st_Viscount_Chelmsford "Frederic Thesiger, 1st Viscount Chelmsford"), viceroy of India, who cautioned the British Government to be more responsive to Indian public opinion

In 1916, in the face of new strength demonstrated by the nationalists with the signing of the [Lucknow Pact](/wiki/Lucknow_Pact "Lucknow Pact") and the founding of the [Home Rule leagues](/wiki/Indian_Home_Rule_movement "Indian Home Rule movement"), and the realisation, after the disaster in the [Mesopotamian campaign](/wiki/Mesopotamian_campaign "Mesopotamian campaign"), that the war would likely last longer, the new viceroy, [Lord Chelmsford](/wiki/Frederic_Thesiger,_1st_Viscount_Chelmsford "Frederic Thesiger, 1st Viscount Chelmsford"), cautioned that the Government of India needed to be more responsive to Indian opinion.[[82]](#cite_note-brown-p203-204-89) Towards the end of the year, after discussions with the government in London, he suggested that the British demonstrate their good faith—in light of the Indian war role—through a number of public actions, including awards of titles and honours to princes, granting of commissions in the army to Indians, and removal of the much-reviled cotton excise duty, but, most importantly, an announcement of Britain's future plans for India and an indication of some concrete steps. After more discussion, in August 1917, the new Liberal secretary of state for India, [Edwin Montagu](/wiki/Edwin_Montagu "Edwin Montagu"), announced the British aim of "increasing association of Indians in every branch of the administration, and the gradual development of self-governing institutions, with a view to the progressive realisation of responsible government in India as an integral part of the British Empire".[[82]](#cite_note-brown-p203-204-89) Although the plan envisioned limited self-government at first only in the provinces—with India emphatically within the British Empire—it represented the first British proposal for any form of representative government in a non-white colony.

Montagu and Chelmsford presented their report in July 1918 after a long fact-finding trip through India the previous winter.[[83]](#cite_note-brown-p205-207-90) After more discussion by the government and parliament in Britain, and another tour by the Franchise and Functions Committee for the purpose of identifying who among the Indian population could vote in future elections, the [Government of India Act 1919](/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1919 "Government of India Act 1919") (also known as the [Montagu–Chelmsford Reforms](/wiki/Montagu%E2%80%93Chelmsford_Reforms "Montagu–Chelmsford Reforms")) was passed in December 1919.[[83]](#cite_note-brown-p205-207-90) The new Act enlarged both the provincial and [Imperial](/wiki/Imperial_Legislative_Council "Imperial Legislative Council") legislative councils and repealed the Government of India's recourse to the "official majority" in unfavourable votes.[[83]](#cite_note-brown-p205-207-90) Although departments like defence, foreign affairs, criminal law, communications, and income-tax were retained by the [Viceroy](/wiki/Governor-General_of_India "Governor-General of India") and the central government in New Delhi, other departments like public health, education, land-revenue, local self-government were transferred to the provinces.[[83]](#cite_note-brown-p205-207-90) The provinces themselves were now to be administered under a new [diarchical](/wiki/Diarchy "Diarchy") system, whereby some areas like education, agriculture, infrastructure development, and local self-government became the preserve of Indian ministers and legislatures, and ultimately the Indian electorates, while others like irrigation, land-revenue, police, prisons, and control of media remained within the purview of the British governor and his executive council.[[83]](#cite_note-brown-p205-207-90) The new Act also made it easier for Indians to be admitted into the civil services and the army officer corps.

A greater number of Indians were now enfranchised, although, for voting at the national level, they constituted only 10% of the total adult male population, many of whom were still illiterate.[[83]](#cite_note-brown-p205-207-90) In the provincial legislatures, the British continued to exercise some control by setting aside seats for special interests they considered cooperative or useful. In particular, rural candidates, generally sympathetic to British rule and less confrontational, were assigned more seats than their urban counterparts.[[83]](#cite_note-brown-p205-207-90) Seats were also reserved for non-Brahmins, landowners, businessmen, and college graduates. The principal of "communal representation", an integral part of the [Minto–Morley Reforms](/wiki/Minto%E2%80%93Morley_Reforms "Minto–Morley Reforms"), and more recently of the Congress-Muslim League Lucknow Pact, was reaffirmed, with seats being reserved for Muslims, [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikhs "Sikhs"), [Indian Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_India "Christianity in India"), [Anglo-Indians](/wiki/Anglo-Indians "Anglo-Indians"), and domiciled Europeans, in both provincial and Imperial legislative councils.[[83]](#cite_note-brown-p205-207-90) The Montagu–Chelmsford reforms offered Indians the most significant opportunity yet for exercising legislative power, especially at the provincial level; however, that opportunity was also restricted by the still limited number of eligible voters, by the small budgets available to provincial legislatures, and by the presence of rural and special interest seats that were seen as instruments of British control.[[83]](#cite_note-brown-p205-207-90) Its scope was unsatisfactory to the Indian political leadership, famously expressed by [Annie Besant](/wiki/Annie_Besant "Annie Besant") as something "unworthy of England to offer and India to accept".[[84]](#cite_note-Chhabra2-91)

### 1917–1919: Rowlatt Act

See also: [Rowlatt Act](/wiki/Rowlatt_Act "Rowlatt Act")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Sir_Sidney_Arthur_Taylor_Rowlatt_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Sir_Sidney_Arthur_Taylor_Rowlatt_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Sidney_Arthur_Taylor_Rowlatt_(cropped).jpg)

[Sidney Rowlatt](/wiki/Sidney_Rowlatt "Sidney Rowlatt"), the British judge under whose chairmanship the [Rowlatt Committee](/wiki/Rowlatt_Committee "Rowlatt Committee") recommended [stricter anti-sedition laws](/wiki/Rowlatt_Act "Rowlatt Act")

In 1917, as Montagu and Chelmsford were compiling their report, a committee chaired by a British judge, [Sidney Rowlatt](/wiki/Sidney_Rowlatt "Sidney Rowlatt"), and was tasked with investigating "revolutionary conspiracies", with the unstated goal of extending the government's wartime powers.[[82]](#cite_note-brown-p203-204-89) The [Rowlatt Committee](/wiki/Rowlatt_Committee "Rowlatt Committee") comprised four British and two Indian members, including [Sir Basil Scott](/wiki/Basil_Scott "Basil Scott") and [Diwan Bahadur Sir C. V. Kumaraswami Sastri](/wiki/C._V._Kumaraswami_Sastri "C. V. Kumaraswami Sastri"), the present and future Chief Justices of the [High Court of Bombay](/wiki/High_Court_of_Bombay "High Court of Bombay") and the [High Court of Madras](/wiki/High_Court_of_Madras "High Court of Madras"). It presented its report in July 1918 and identified three regions of conspiratorial insurgency: [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), the [Bombay presidency](/wiki/Bombay_presidency "Bombay presidency"), and the [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India").[[82]](#cite_note-brown-p203-204-89) To combat subversive acts in these regions, the committee unanimously recommended that the government use emergency powers akin to its wartime authority, which included the ability to try cases of sedition by a panel of three judges and without juries, exaction of securities from suspects, governmental overseeing of residences of suspects,[[82]](#cite_note-brown-p203-204-89) and the power for provincial governments to arrest and detain suspects in short-term detention facilities and without trial.[[85]](#cite_note-spear190-92)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Rowlatt_bills1919.gif/250px-Rowlatt_bills1919.gif)](/wiki/File:Rowlatt_bills1919.gif)

Headlines about the Rowlatt Bills (1919) from a nationalist newspaper in India. Although all non-official Indians on the Legislative Council voted against the Rowlatt Bills, the government was able to force their passage by using its majority.[[85]](#cite_note-spear190-92)

With the end of World War I, there was also a change in the economic climate. By the end of 1919, 1.5 million Indians had served in the armed services in either combatant or non-combatant roles, and India had provided £146 million in revenue for the war.[[86]](#cite_note-brown195-196-93) The increased taxes coupled with disruptions in both domestic and international trade had the effect of approximately doubling the index of overall prices in India between 1914 and 1920.[[86]](#cite_note-brown195-196-93) Returning war veterans, especially in the Punjab, created a growing unemployment crisis,[[87]](#cite_note-stein304-94) and post-war inflation led to food riots in Bombay, Madras, and Bengal provinces,[[87]](#cite_note-stein304-94) a situation that was made only worse by the failure of the 1918–19 monsoon and by profiteering and speculation.[[86]](#cite_note-brown195-196-93) The global influenza epidemic and the [Bolshevik Revolution](/wiki/Bolshevik_Revolution "Bolshevik Revolution") of 1917 added to the general jitters; the former among the population already experiencing economic woes,[[87]](#cite_note-stein304-94) and the latter among government officials, fearing a similar revolution in India.[[88]](#cite_note-95)

To combat what it saw as a coming crisis, the government now drafted the Rowlatt committee's recommendations into two [Rowlatt Bills](/wiki/Rowlatt_Acts "Rowlatt Acts").[[85]](#cite_note-spear190-92) Although the bills were authorised for legislative consideration by Edwin Montagu, they were done so unwillingly, with the accompanying declaration, "I loathe the suggestion at first sight of preserving the Defence of India Act in peacetime to such an extent as Rowlatt and his friends think necessary."[[82]](#cite_note-brown-p203-204-89) In the ensuing discussion and vote in the Imperial Legislative Council, all Indian members voiced opposition to the bills. The Government of India was, nevertheless, able to use of its "official majority" to ensure passage of the bills early in 1919.[[82]](#cite_note-brown-p203-204-89) However, what it passed, in deference to the Indian opposition, was a lesser version of the first bill, which now allowed extrajudicial powers, but for a period of exactly three years and for the prosecution solely of "anarchical and revolutionary movements", dropping entirely the second bill involving modification the [Indian Penal Code](/wiki/Indian_Penal_Code "Indian Penal Code").[[82]](#cite_note-brown-p203-204-89) Even so, when it was passed, the new [Rowlatt Act](/wiki/Rowlatt_Act "Rowlatt Act") aroused widespread indignation throughout India, and brought Gandhi to the forefront of the nationalist movement.[[85]](#cite_note-spear190-92)

### 1919–1929: Jallianwala, non-cooperation

See also: [Jallianwala Bagh massacre](/wiki/Jallianwala_Bagh_massacre "Jallianwala Bagh massacre"), [Non-cooperation movement (1909–22)](/wiki/Non-cooperation_movement_(1909%E2%80%9322) "Non-cooperation movement (1909–22)"), [Simon Commission](/wiki/Simon_Commission "Simon Commission"), [Purna Swaraj](/wiki/Purna_Swaraj "Purna Swaraj"), [Round Table Conferences](/wiki/Round_Table_Conferences "Round Table Conferences"), and [Government of India Act 1935](/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1935 "Government of India Act 1935")

* [![Gandhi with Besant en route to a meeting in Madras in September 1921. Earlier, in Madurai, on 21 September 1921, Gandhi had adopted the loin-cloth in identification with India's poor.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Gandhi_besant_madras1921.jpg/120px-Gandhi_besant_madras1921.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gandhi_besant_madras1921.jpg "Gandhi with Besant en route to a meeting in Madras in September 1921. Earlier, in Madurai, on 21 September 1921, Gandhi had adopted the loin-cloth in identification with India's poor.")

  Gandhi with Besant *en route* to a meeting in [Madras](/wiki/Madras "Madras") in September 1921. Earlier, in [Madurai](/wiki/Madurai "Madurai"), on 21 September 1921, Gandhi had adopted the [loin-cloth](/wiki/Loin-cloth "Loin-cloth") in identification with India's poor.
