The [Jallianwala Bagh massacre](/wiki/Jallianwala_Bagh_massacre "Jallianwala Bagh massacre") or "Amritsar massacre", took place in the [Jallianwala Bagh](/wiki/Jallianwala_Bagh "Jallianwala Bagh") public garden in the predominantly Sikh northern city of [Amritsar](/wiki/Amritsar "Amritsar"). After days of unrest Brigadier-General [Reginald E.H. Dyer](/wiki/Reginald_Dyer "Reginald Dyer") forbade public meetings and on Sunday 13 April 1919 fifty British Indian Army soldiers commanded by Dyer began shooting at an unarmed gathering of thousands of men, women, and children without warning. Casualty estimates vary widely, with the Government of India reporting 379 dead, with 1,100 wounded.[[89]](#cite_note-96) The [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") estimated three times the number of dead. Dyer was removed from duty but he became a celebrated hero in Britain among people with connections to the Raj.[[90]](#cite_note-97) Historians consider the episode was a decisive step towards the end of British rule in India.[[91]](#cite_note-98)

In 1920, after the British government refused to back down, Gandhi began his campaign of [non-cooperation](/wiki/Non-cooperation_movement_(1909%E2%80%9322) "Non-cooperation movement (1909–22)"), prompting many Indians to return British awards and honours, to resign from the civil services, and to again boycott British goods. In addition, Gandhi reorganised the Congress, transforming it into a mass movement and opening its membership to even the poorest Indians. Although Gandhi halted the non-cooperation movement in 1922 after the violent [incident at Chauri Chaura](/wiki/Chauri_Chaura_incident "Chauri Chaura incident"), the movement revived again, in the mid-1920s.

The visit, in 1928, of the British [Simon Commission](/wiki/Simon_Commission "Simon Commission"), charged with instituting constitutional reform in India, resulted in widespread protests throughout the country.[[92]](#cite_note-markovits-simon-bhagat-99) Earlier, in 1925, non-violent protests of the Congress had resumed too, this time in Gujarat, and led by Patel, who organised farmers to refuse payment of increased land taxes; the success of this protest, the [Bardoli Satyagraha](/wiki/Bardoli_Satyagraha "Bardoli Satyagraha"), brought Gandhi back into the fold of active politics.[[92]](#cite_note-markovits-simon-bhagat-99)

At its annual session in [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), the Indian National Congress, under the presidency of [Jawaharlal Nehru](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru "Jawaharlal Nehru"), issued a demand for *[Purna Swaraj](/wiki/Purna_Swaraj "Purna Swaraj")* ([Hindustani language](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language"): "complete independence"), or Purna Swarajya. The declaration was drafted by the [Congress Working Committee](/wiki/Congress_Working_Committee "Congress Working Committee"), which included Gandhi, Nehru, Patel, and [Chakravarthi Rajagopalachari](/wiki/Chakravarthi_Rajagopalachari "Chakravarthi Rajagopalachari"). Gandhi subsequently led an expanded movement of civil disobedience, culminating in 1930 with the [Salt Satyagraha](/wiki/Salt_Satyagraha "Salt Satyagraha"), in which thousands of Indians defied the tax on salt, by marching to the sea and making their own salt by evaporating seawater. Although, many, including Gandhi, were arrested, the British government eventually gave in, and in 1931 Gandhi travelled to London to negotiate new reform at the [Round Table Conferences](/wiki/Round_Table_Conferences "Round Table Conferences").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Government of India Act, 1935

In local terms, British control rested on the [Indian Civil Service](/wiki/Indian_Civil_Service "Indian Civil Service") (ICS), but it faced growing difficulties. Fewer and fewer young men in Britain were interested in joining, and the continuing distrust of Indians resulted in a declining base in terms of quality and quantity. By 1945 Indians were numerically dominant in the ICS and at issue was divided loyalty between the Empire and independence.[[93]](#cite_note-100) The finances of the Raj depended on land taxes, and these became problematic in the 1930s. Epstein argues that after 1919 it became harder and harder to collect the land revenue. The Raj's suppression of civil disobedience after 1934 temporarily increased the power of the revenue agents but after 1937 they were forced by the new Congress-controlled provincial governments to hand back confiscated land. Again the outbreak of war strengthened them, in the face of the [Quit India](/wiki/Quit_India "Quit India") movement the revenue collectors had to rely on military force and by 1946–47 direct British control was rapidly disappearing in much of the countryside.[[94]](#cite_note-101)

In 1935, after the Round Table Conferences, Parliament passed the [Government of India Act 1935](/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1935 "Government of India Act 1935"), which authorised the establishment of independent legislative assemblies in all provinces of British India, the creation of a central government incorporating both the British provinces and the princely states, and the protection of Muslim minorities. The future [Constitution of independent India](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India") was based on this act.[[95]](#cite_note-102) However, it divided the electorate into 19 religious and social categories, e.g., Muslims, Sikhs, Indian Christians, Depressed Classes, Landholders, Commerce and Industry, Europeans, Anglo-Indians, etc., each of which was given separate representation in the Provincial Legislative Assemblies. A voter could cast a vote only for candidates in his own category.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The 1935 Act provided for more autonomy for Indian provinces, with the goal of cooling off nationalist sentiment. The act provided for a national parliament and an executive branch under the purview of the British government, but the rulers of the princely states managed to block its implementation. These states remained under the full control of their hereditary rulers, with no popular government. To prepare for elections Congress built up its grass roots membership from 473,000 in 1935 to 4.5 million in 1939.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrendon2008394-103)

In the [1937 elections](/wiki/Indian_Provincial_Elections,_1937 "Indian Provincial Elections, 1937") Congress won victories in seven of the eleven provinces of British India.[[97]](#cite_note-low-1935-elections-104) Congress governments, with wide powers, were formed in these provinces. The widespread voter support for the Indian National Congress surprised Raj officials, who previously had seen the Congress as a small elitist body.[[98]](#cite_note-105) The British separated Burma Province from British India in 1937 and granted the colony a new constitution calling for a fully elected assembly, with many powers given to the Burmese, but this proved to be a divisive issue as a ploy to exclude Burmese from any further Indian reforms.[[99]](#cite_note-106)

### 1939–1945: World War II

See also: [India in World War II](/wiki/India_in_World_War_II "India in World War II"), [Indian Army during World War II](/wiki/Indian_Army_during_World_War_II "Indian Army during World War II"), [Cripps Mission](/wiki/Cripps_Mission "Cripps Mission"), and [Quit India Movement](/wiki/Quit_India_Movement "Quit India Movement")

With the outbreak of World War II in 1939, the viceroy, [Lord Linlithgow](/wiki/Victor_Hope,_2nd_Marquess_of_Linlithgow "Victor Hope, 2nd Marquess of Linlithgow"), declared war on India's behalf without consulting Indian leaders, leading the Congress provincial ministries to resign in protest. The Muslim League, in contrast, supported Britain in the war effort and maintained its control of the government in three major provinces, Bengal, Sind and the Punjab.[[20]](#cite_note-British_Raj_Independence_movement-26)

While the Muslim League had been a small elite group in 1927 with only 1300 members, it grew rapidly once it became an organisation that reached out to the masses, reaching 500,000 members in Bengal in 1944, 200,000 in Punjab, and hundreds of thousands elsewhere.[[100]](#cite_note-India_and_Pakistan_independence-107) Jinnah now was well positioned to negotiate with the British from a position of power.[[101]](#cite_note-108) Jinnah repeatedly warned that Muslims would be unfairly treated in an independent India dominated by the Congress. On 24 March 1940 in Lahore, the League passed the "[Lahore Resolution](/wiki/Lahore_Resolution "Lahore Resolution")", demanding that, "the areas in which the Muslims are numerically in majority as in the North-Western and Eastern zones of India should be grouped to constitute independent states in which the constituent units shall be autonomous and sovereign."[[102]](#cite_note-109) Although there were other important national Muslim politicians such as Congress leader [Ab'ul Kalam Azad](/wiki/Ab%27ul_Kalam_Azad "Ab'ul Kalam Azad"), and influential regional Muslim politicians such as [A. K. Fazlul Huq](/wiki/A._K._Fazlul_Huq "A. K. Fazlul Huq") of the leftist [Krishak Praja Party](/wiki/Krishak_Praja_Party "Krishak Praja Party") in Bengal, [Fazl-i-Hussain](/wiki/Fazl-i-Hussain "Fazl-i-Hussain") of the landlord-dominated [Punjab Unionist Party](/wiki/Unionist_Muslim_League "Unionist Muslim League"), and [Abd al-Ghaffar Khan](/wiki/Abdul_Ghaffar_Khan "Abdul Ghaffar Khan") of the pro-Congress [Khudai Khidmatgar](/wiki/Khudai_Khidmatgar "Khudai Khidmatgar") (popularly, "red shirts") in the [North West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province_(1901%E2%80%931955) "North-West Frontier Province (1901–1955)"),[[103]](#cite_note-110) the British, over the next six years, were to increasingly see the League as the main representative of Muslim India.

The Congress was secular and strongly opposed to having any religious state.[[100]](#cite_note-India_and_Pakistan_independence-107) It insisted there was a natural unity to India, and repeatedly blamed the British for "divide and rule" tactics based on prompting Muslims to think of themselves as alien from Hindus.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Jinnah rejected the notion of a united India, and emphasised that religious communities were more basic than an artificial nationalism. He proclaimed the [Two-Nation Theory](/wiki/Two-Nation_Theory "Two-Nation Theory"),[[104]](#cite_note-111) stating at Lahore on 23 March 1940:

> [Islam and Hinduism] are not religions in the strict sense of the word, but are, in fact, different and distinct social orders and it is a dream that the Hindus and Muslims can ever evolve a common nationality ... The Hindu and Muslim belong to two different religions, philosophies, social customs and literature [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*]. They neither intermarry nor interdine together and indeed they belong to two different civilizations which are based mainly on conflicting ideas and conceptions. Their aspects on life and of life are different ... To yoke together two such nations under a single state, one as a numerical minority and the other as a majority must lead to growing discontent and final destruction of any fabric that may be so built up for the government of such a state.[[105]](#cite_note-112)

While the regular Indian army in 1939 included about 220,000 native troops, it expanded tenfold during the war,[[106]](#cite_note-113) and small naval and air force units were created. Over two million Indians volunteered for military service in the British Army. They played a major role in numerous campaigns, especially in the Middle East and North Africa. Casualties were moderate (in terms of the world war), with 24,000 killed; 64,000 wounded; 12,000 missing (probably dead), and 60,000 captured at Singapore in 1942.[[107]](#cite_note-114)

London paid most of the cost of the Indian Army, which had the effect of erasing India's national debt; it ended the war with a surplus of £1,300 million. In addition, heavy British spending on munitions produced in India (such as uniforms, rifles, machine-guns, field artillery, and ammunition) led to a rapid expansion of industrial output, such as textiles (up 16%), steel (up 18%), and chemicals (up 30%). Small warships were built, and an aircraft factory opened in [Bangalore](/wiki/Bengaluru "Bengaluru"). The railway system, with 700,000 employees, was taxed to the limit as demand for transportation soared.[[108]](#cite_note-115)

The British government sent the [Cripps mission](/wiki/Cripps_mission "Cripps mission") in 1942 to secure Indian nationalists' co-operation in the war effort in exchange for a promise of independence as soon as the war ended. Top officials in Britain, most notably Prime Minister [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"), did not support the Cripps Mission and negotiations with the Congress soon broke down.[[109]](#cite_note-116)

Congress launched the [Quit India Movement](/wiki/Quit_India_Movement "Quit India Movement") in July 1942 demanding the immediate withdrawal of the British from India or face nationwide civil disobedience. On 8 August the Raj arrested all national, provincial and local Congress leaders, holding tens of thousands of them until 1945. The country erupted in violent demonstrations led by students and later by peasant political groups, especially in Eastern [United Provinces](/wiki/United_Provinces_of_Agra_and_Oudh "United Provinces of Agra and Oudh"), Bihar, and western Bengal. The large wartime British Army presence crushed the movement in a little more than six weeks;[[110]](#cite_note-metcalf-quit-india-117) nonetheless, a portion of the movement formed for a time an underground provisional government on the border with Nepal.[[110]](#cite_note-metcalf-quit-india-117) In other parts of India, the movement was less spontaneous and the protest less intensive; however, it lasted sporadically into the summer of 1943.[[111]](#cite_note-Bandypadhya420-118)

Earlier, [Subhas Chandra Bose](/wiki/Subhas_Chandra_Bose "Subhas Chandra Bose"), who had been a leader of the younger, radical, wing of the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") in the late 1920s and 1930s, had risen to become Congress President from 1938 to 1939.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010305,_325-119) However, he was ousted from the Congress in 1939 following differences with the high command,[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELow2002297-120) and subsequently placed under house arrest by the British before escaping from India in early 1941.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELow2002313-121) He turned to [Nazi Germany and Imperial Japan](/wiki/Axis_powers_of_World_War_II "Axis powers of World War II") for help in gaining India's independence by force.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELow199331–31-122) With Japanese support, he organised the [Indian National Army](/wiki/Indian_National_Army "Indian National Army"), composed largely of Indian soldiers of the British Indian Army who had been captured by the Japanese in the [Battle of Singapore](/wiki/Battle_of_Singapore "Battle of Singapore"). As the war turned against them, the Japanese came to support a number of [puppet and provisional governments](/wiki/Collaboration_with_Imperial_Japan "Collaboration with Imperial Japan") in the captured regions, including those in Burma, the Philippines and Vietnam, and in addition, the Provisional Government of [Azad Hind](/wiki/Azad_Hind "Azad Hind"), presided by Bose.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELow199331–31-122)

Bose's effort, however, was short-lived. In mid-1944 the British Army first halted and then reversed the Japanese [U-Go offensive](/wiki/Operation_U-Go "Operation U-Go"), beginning the successful part of the [Burma Campaign](/wiki/Burma_Campaign "Burma Campaign"). Bose's Indian National Army largely disintegrated during the subsequent fighting in Burma, with its remaining elements surrendering with the recapture of Singapore in September 1945.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Bose died in August from third degree burns received after attempting to escape in an overloaded Japanese plane which crashed in Taiwan,[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaylyHarper200721-123)[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolpert200669-124) which many Indians believe did not happen.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBandyopadhyay2004427-125)[[119]](#cite_note-sugata-bose-p320-126) Although Bose was unsuccessful, he roused patriotic feelings in India.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010345-127)

### 1946–1947: Independence, partition

See also: [Partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") and [Interim Government of India](/wiki/Interim_Government_of_India "Interim Government of India")

In January 1946, a number of mutinies broke out in the armed services, starting with that of RAF servicemen frustrated with their slow repatriation to Britain.[[121]](#cite_note-judd-mutiny-128) The mutinies came to a head with [mutiny of the Royal Indian Navy](/wiki/The_Royal_Indian_Navy_Mutiny "The Royal Indian Navy Mutiny") in Bombay in February 1946, followed by others in [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta"), [Madras](/wiki/Madras "Madras"), and [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi"). Although the mutinies were rapidly suppressed, they had the effect of spurring the new Labour government in Britain to action, and leading to the Cabinet Mission to India led by the secretary of state for India, [Lord Pethick Lawrence](/wiki/Frederick_Pethick-Lawrence,_1st_Baron_Pethick-Lawrence "Frederick Pethick-Lawrence, 1st Baron Pethick-Lawrence"), and including [Sir Stafford Cripps](/wiki/Stafford_Cripps "Stafford Cripps"), who had visited four years before.[[121]](#cite_note-judd-mutiny-128)

Also in early 1946, new elections were called in India. Earlier, at the end of the war in 1945, the colonial government had announced the [public trial](/wiki/Indian_National_Army_trials "Indian National Army trials") of three senior officers of Bose's defeated Indian National Army who stood accused of treason. Now as the trials began, the Congress leadership, although ambivalent towards the INA, chose to defend the accused officers.[[122]](#cite_note-129) The subsequent convictions of the officers, the public outcry against the convictions, and the eventual remission of the sentences, created positive propaganda for the Congress, which only helped in the party's subsequent electoral victories in eight of the eleven provinces.[[123]](#cite_note-130) The negotiations between the Congress and the Muslim League, however, stumbled over the issue of the partition. Jinnah proclaimed 16 August 1946, [Direct Action Day](/wiki/Direct_Action_Day "Direct Action Day"), with the stated goal of highlighting, peacefully, the demand for a Muslim homeland in [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India"). The following day Hindu-Muslim riots broke out in Calcutta and quickly spread throughout British India. Although the Government of India and the Congress were both shaken by the course of events, in September, a Congress-led interim government was installed, with Jawaharlal Nehru as united India's prime minister.[[124]](#cite_note-Gopal1976-131)

Later that year, the [British Exchequer](/wiki/Exchequer "Exchequer") exhausted by the recently concluded World War II, and the [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") government conscious that it had neither the mandate at home, the international support, nor the reliability of [native forces](/wiki/British_Indian_Army "British Indian Army") for continuing to control an increasingly restless British India,[[125]](#cite_note-Hyam106-132)[[126]](#cite_note-133) decided to end British rule of India, and in early 1947 Britain announced its intention of transferring power no later than June 1948.[[20]](#cite_note-British_Raj_Independence_movement-26)

As independence approached, the violence between Hindus and Muslims in the provinces of Punjab and Bengal continued unabated. With the British army unprepared for the potential for increased violence, the new viceroy, [Louis Mountbatten](/wiki/Louis_Mountbatten "Louis Mountbatten"), advanced the date for the transfer of power, allowing less than six months for a mutually agreed plan for independence.[[127]](#cite_note-Indian_Independence_movement-134)[[20]](#cite_note-British_Raj_Independence_movement-26) With the [partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India"), the end of the British rule in India in August 1947 saw the creation of two separate states of India and Pakistan.[[128]](#cite_note-135)

On 15 August 1947, the new [Dominion of Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan") (later [Islamic Republic of Pakistan](/wiki/Islamic_Republic_of_Pakistan "Islamic Republic of Pakistan")), with Muhammad Ali Jinnah as the governor-general; and the [Dominion of India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India"), (later [Republic of India](/wiki/Republic_of_India "Republic of India")) with [Jawaharlal Nehru](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru "Jawaharlal Nehru") as the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_India "Prime Minister of India"), and the viceroy, [Louis Mountbatten](/wiki/Louis_Mountbatten "Louis Mountbatten"), staying on as its first governor-general came into being; with official ceremonies taking place in [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi") on 14 August and [New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi") on 15 August. This was done so that Mountbatten could attend both ceremonies.[[129]](#cite_note-136)

The great majority of Indians remained in place with independence, but in border areas millions of people (Muslim, Sikh, and Hindu) relocated across the [newly drawn borders](/wiki/Radcliffe_Line "Radcliffe Line"). In Punjab, where the new border lines divided the Sikh regions in half, there was much bloodshed; in Bengal and Bihar, where Gandhi's presence assuaged communal tempers, the violence was more limited. In all, somewhere between 250,000 and 500,000 people on both sides of the new borders, among both the refugee and resident populations of the three faiths, died in the violence.[[130]](#cite_note-137)

## British India and the princely states

Main articles: [Presidencies and provinces of British India](/wiki/Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India "Presidencies and provinces of British India"), [Princely state](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state"), and [Subdivisions of British India](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_British_India "Subdivisions of British India")

India during the British Raj was made up of two types of territory: *British India* and the *Native States* (or *[Princely States](/wiki/Princely_State "Princely State")*).[[142]](#cite_note-WDL-150) In its [Interpretation Act 1889](/wiki/Interpretation_Act_1889 "Interpretation Act 1889"), the [British Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") adopted the following definitions in Section 18:

> (4.) The expression "British India" shall mean all territories and places within Her Majesty's dominions which are for the time being governed by Her Majesty through the Governor-General of India or through any governor or other officer subordinates to the Governor-General of India.
>
> (5.) The expression "India" shall mean British India together with any territories of any native prince or chief under the suzerainty of Her Majesty exercised through the Governor-General of India, or through any governor or other officer subordinates to the Governor-General of India.[[2]](#cite_note-Interpretation_Act_1889-4)

In general, the term "British India" had been used (and is still used) to refer also to the regions under [the rule of the British East India Company](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India") in India from 1600 to 1858.[[143]](#cite_note-151) The term has also been used to refer to the "British in India".[[144]](#cite_note-152)

The terms "Indian Empire" and "Empire of India" (like the term "British Empire") were not used in legislation. The monarch was officially known as Empress or Emperor of India and the term was often used in Queen Victoria's [Queen's Speeches](/wiki/Speech_from_the_throne "Speech from the throne") and Prorogation Speeches. In addition, an [order of knighthood](/wiki/Chivalric_order "Chivalric order"), [the Most Eminent Order of the Indian Empire](/wiki/Order_of_the_Indian_Empire "Order of the Indian Empire"), was set up in 1878.

[Suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty "Suzerainty") over 175 princely states, some of the largest and most important, was exercised (in the name of the [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown")) by the central government of British India under the [viceroy](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy"); the remaining approximately 500 states were dependents of the provincial governments of British India under a governor, lieutenant-governor, or chief commissioner (as the case might have been).[[145]](#cite_note-igi-60-153) A clear distinction between "dominion" and "suzerainty" was supplied by the jurisdiction of the courts of law: the law of British India rested upon the laws passed by the British Parliament and the legislative powers those laws vested in the various governments of British India, both central and local; in contrast, the courts of the Princely States existed under the authority of the respective rulers of those states.[[145]](#cite_note-igi-60-153)

### Princely states

Main article: [Princely state](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state")

A Princely State, also called a Native State or an Indian State, was a British [vassal state](/wiki/Vassal_state "Vassal state") in India with an indigenous nominal Indian ruler, subject to a [subsidiary alliance](/wiki/Subsidiary_alliance "Subsidiary alliance").[[148]](#cite_note-Markovits2004-156) There were 565 princely states when India and Pakistan became independent from Britain in August 1947. The princely states did not form a part of [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") (i.e. the presidencies and provinces), as they were not directly under British rule. The larger ones had treaties with Britain that specified which rights the princes had; in the smaller ones the princes had few rights. Within the princely states external affairs, defence and most communications were under British control.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The British also exercised a general influence over the states' internal politics, in part through the granting or withholding of recognition of individual rulers. Although there were nearly 600 princely states, the great majority were very small and contracted out the business of government to the British. Some two hundred of the states had an area of less than 25 square kilometres (10 square miles).[[148]](#cite_note-Markovits2004-156) The last vestige of the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") in Delhi which was under [Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") authority prior to the advent of British Raj was finally abolished and seized by the [Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") in the aftermath of the [Sepoy Mutiny of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857") for its support to the rebellion.[[149]](#cite_note-157)[[150]](#cite_note-158)

The princely states were grouped into [agencies](/wiki/Agencies_of_British_India "Agencies of British India") and [residencies](/wiki/Residencies_of_British_India "Residencies of British India").

### Organisation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/1stViscountHalifax.jpg/250px-1stViscountHalifax.jpg)](/wiki/File:1stViscountHalifax.jpg)

[Sir Charles Wood](/wiki/Charles_Wood,_1st_Viscount_Halifax "Charles Wood, 1st Viscount Halifax") (1800–1885) was [President of the Board of Control](/wiki/President_of_the_Board_of_Control "President of the Board of Control") of the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") from 1852 to 1855; he shaped British education policy in India, and was [Secretary of State for India](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_India "Secretary of State for India") from 1859 to 1866.

Following the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857") (usually called the Indian Mutiny by the British), the [Government of India Act 1858](/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1858 "Government of India Act 1858") made changes in the governance of India at three levels:

1. in the imperial government in London,
2. in the central government in [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta"), and
3. in the provincial governments in the presidencies (and later in the provinces).[[151]](#cite_note-159)

In London, it provided for a cabinet-level [Secretary of State for India](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_India "Secretary of State for India") and a fifteen-member [Council of India](/wiki/Council_of_India "Council of India"), whose members were required, as one prerequisite of membership, to have spent at least ten years in India and to have done so no more than ten years before.[[152]](#cite_note-160) Although the secretary of state formulated the policy instructions to be communicated to India, he was required in most instances to consult the Council, but especially so in matters relating to spending of Indian revenues. The Act envisaged a system of "double government" in which the Council ideally served both as a check on excesses in imperial policy-making and as a body of up-to-date expertise on India. However, the secretary of state also had special emergency powers that allowed him to make unilateral decisions, and, in reality, the Council's expertise was sometimes outdated.[[153]](#cite_note-161) From 1858 until 1947, twenty-seven individuals served as Secretary of State for India and directed the [India Office](/wiki/India_Office "India Office"); these included: Sir [Charles Wood](/wiki/Charles_Wood,_1st_Viscount_Halifax "Charles Wood, 1st Viscount Halifax") (1859–1866), the [Marquess of Salisbury](/wiki/Robert_Gascoyne-Cecil,_3rd_Marquess_of_Salisbury "Robert Gascoyne-Cecil, 3rd Marquess of Salisbury") (1874–1878; later British prime minister), [John Morley](/wiki/John_Morley "John Morley") (1905–1910; initiator of the [Minto–Morley Reforms](/wiki/Minto%E2%80%93Morley_Reforms "Minto–Morley Reforms")), [E. S. Montagu](/wiki/Edwin_Montagu "Edwin Montagu") (1917–1922; an architect of the [Montagu–Chelmsford Reforms](/wiki/Montagu%E2%80%93Chelmsford_Reforms "Montagu–Chelmsford Reforms")), and [Frederick Pethick-Lawrence](/wiki/Frederick_Pethick-Lawrence "Frederick Pethick-Lawrence") (1945–1947; head of the [1946 Cabinet Mission to India](/wiki/1946_Cabinet_Mission_to_India "1946 Cabinet Mission to India")). The size of the Advisory Council was reduced over the next half-century, but its powers remained unchanged. In 1907, for the first time, two Indians were appointed to the Council.[[154]](#cite_note-moore-426-162) They were [K.G. Gupta](/wiki/K.G._Gupta "K.G. Gupta") and [Syed Hussain Bilgrami](/wiki/Syed_Hussain_Bilgrami "Syed Hussain Bilgrami").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Lord_Viscount_Canning.jpg/250px-Lord_Viscount_Canning.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord_Viscount_Canning.jpg)

[Lord Canning](/wiki/Lord_Canning "Lord Canning"), the last [Governor-General of India](/wiki/Governor-General_of_India "Governor-General of India") under [Company rule](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India") and the first [viceroy of India](/wiki/Viceroy_of_India "Viceroy of India") under Crown rule

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Robert-Gascoyne-Cecil-3rd-Marquess-of-Salisbury_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Robert-Gascoyne-Cecil-3rd-Marquess-of-Salisbury_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robert-Gascoyne-Cecil-3rd-Marquess-of-Salisbury_(cropped).jpg)

[Lord Salisbury](/wiki/Robert_Gascoyne-Cecil,_3rd_Marquess_of_Salisbury "Robert Gascoyne-Cecil, 3rd Marquess of Salisbury") was [Secretary of State for India](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_India "Secretary of State for India") from 1874 to 1878.

In Calcutta, the [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-General_of_India "Governor-General of India") remained head of the Government of India and now was more commonly called the viceroy on account of his secondary role as the Crown's representative to the nominally sovereign princely states; he was, however, now responsible to the secretary of state in London and through him to Parliament. A system of "double government" had already been in place during the Company's rule in India from the time of Pitt's [India Act of 1784](/wiki/Pitt%27s_India_Act "Pitt's India Act"). The governor-general in the capital, Calcutta, and the governor in a subordinate presidency ([Madras](/wiki/Madras_Presidency "Madras Presidency") or [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay_Presidency "Bombay Presidency")) was each required to consult his advisory council; executive orders in Calcutta, for example, were issued in the name of "Governor-General-in-Council" (*i.e.* the Governor-General with the advice of the Council). The Company's system of "double government" had its critics, since, from the time of the system's inception, there had been intermittent feuding between the governor-general and his Council; still, the Act of 1858 made no major changes in governance.[[154]](#cite_note-moore-426-162) However, in the years immediately thereafter, which were also the years of post-rebellion reconstruction, Viceroy [Lord Canning](/wiki/Charles_Canning,_1st_Earl_Canning "Charles Canning, 1st Earl Canning") found the collective decision making of the Council to be too time-consuming for the pressing tasks ahead, so he requested the "portfolio system" of an [Executive Council](/wiki/Executive_Council_(Commonwealth_countries) "Executive Council (Commonwealth countries)") in which the business of each government department (the "portfolio") was assigned to and became the responsibility of a single council member.[[154]](#cite_note-moore-426-162) Routine departmental decisions were made exclusively by the member, but important decisions required the consent of the governor-general and, in the absence of such consent, required discussion by the entire Executive Council. This innovation in Indian governance was promulgated in the [Indian Councils Act 1861](/wiki/Indian_Councils_Act_1861 "Indian Councils Act 1861").

If the Government of India needed to enact new laws, the Councils Act allowed for a Legislative Council—an expansion of the Executive Council by up to twelve additional members, each appointed to a two-year term—with half the members consisting of British officials of the government (termed *official*) and allowed to vote, and the other half, comprising Indians and domiciled [Britons in India](/wiki/Britons_in_India "Britons in India") (termed *non-official*) and serving only in an advisory capacity.[[155]](#cite_note-163) All laws enacted by Legislative Councils in India, whether by the [Imperial Legislative Council](/wiki/Imperial_Legislative_Council "Imperial Legislative Council") in [Calcutta](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata") or by the provincial ones in [Madras](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai") and [Bombay](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"), required the final assent of the secretary of state in [London](/wiki/London "London"); this prompted Sir Charles Wood, the second secretary of state, to describe the Government of India as "a despotism controlled from home".[[154]](#cite_note-moore-426-162) Moreover, although the appointment of Indians to the Legislative Council was a response to calls after the 1857 rebellion, most notably by [Sayyid Ahmad Khan](/wiki/Sayyid_Ahmad_Khan "Sayyid Ahmad Khan"), for more consultation with Indians, the Indians so appointed were from the landed aristocracy, often chosen for their loyalty, and far from representative.[[156]](#cite_note-164) Even so, the "...  tiny advances in the practice of representative government were intended to provide safety valves for the expression of public opinion, which had been so badly misjudged before the rebellion".[[157]](#cite_note-165) Indian affairs now also came to be more closely examined in the British Parliament and more widely discussed in the British press.[[158]](#cite_note-166)

With the promulgation of the Government of India Act 1935, the Council of India was abolished with effect from 1 April 1937 and a modified system of government enacted. The secretary of state for India represented the Government of India in the UK. He was assisted by a body of advisers numbering from 8–12 individuals, at least half of whom were required to have held office in India for a minimum of 10 years, and had not relinquished office earlier than two years prior to their appointment as advisers to the secretary of state.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinberg1947103–105"India_–_Government_and_Constitution"-167)

The viceroy and governor-general of India, a Crown appointee, typically held office for five years though there was no fixed tenure, and received an annual salary of Rs. 250,800 p.a. (£18,810 p.a.).[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinberg1947103–105"India_–_Government_and_Constitution"-167)[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinberg1947133–34"India_–_Currency,_Weights_and_Measures"-168) He headed the Viceroy's Executive Council, each member of which had responsibility for a department of the central administration. From 1 April 1937, the position of Governor-General in Council, which the viceroy and governor-general concurrently held in the capacity of representing the Crown in relations with the Indian princely states, was replaced by the designation of "HM Representative for the Exercise of the Functions of the Crown in its Relations with the Indian States", or the "Crown Representative". The Executive Council was greatly expanded during the Second World War, and in 1947 comprised 14 members ([secretaries](/wiki/Secretary_to_the_Government_of_India "Secretary to the Government of India")), each of whom earned a salary of Rs. 66,000 p.a. (£4,950 p.a.).

Until 1946, the viceroy held the portfolio for External Affairs and Commonwealth Relations, as well as heading the Political Department in his capacity as the Crown representative. Each department was headed by a [secretary](/wiki/Secretary_to_the_Government_of_India "Secretary to the Government of India") excepting the Railway Department, which was headed by a Chief Commissioner of Railways under a secretary.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinberg1947106–107"India_–_Government_and_Constitution"-169)

The viceroy and governor-general was also the head of the bicameral Indian Legislature, consisting of an upper house (the Council of State) and a lower house (the Legislative Assembly). The viceroy was the head of the Council of State, while the Legislative Assembly, which was first opened in 1921, was headed by an elected president (appointed by the Viceroy from 1921 to 1925). The Council of State consisted of 58 members (32 elected, 26 nominated), while the Legislative Assembly comprised 141 members (26 nominated officials, 13 others nominated and 102 elected). The Council of State existed in five-year periods and the Legislative Assembly for three-year periods, though either could be dissolved earlier or later by the Viceroy. The Indian Legislature was empowered to make laws for all persons resident in British India including all British subjects resident in India, and for all British Indian subjects residing outside India. With the assent of the King-Emperor and after copies of a proposed enactment had been submitted to both houses of the British Parliament, the Viceroy could overrule the legislature and directly enact any measures in the perceived interests of British India or its residents if the need arose.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinberg1947106–107"India_–_Government_and_Constitution"-169)

Effective from 1 April 1936, the Government of India Act created the new provinces of Sind (separated from the Bombay Presidency) and Orissa (separated from the Province of Bihar and Orissa). Burma and Aden became separate Crown Colonies under the Act from 1 April 1937, thereby ceasing to be part of the Indian Empire. From 1937 onwards, British India was divided into 17 administrations: the three Presidencies of Madras, Bombay and Bengal, and the 14 provinces of the United Provinces, Punjab, Bihar, the Central Provinces and Berar, Assam, the North-West Frontier Province (NWFP), Orissa, Sind, British Baluchistan, Delhi, Ajmer-Merwara, Coorg, the Andaman and Nicobar Islands and Panth Piploda. The Presidencies and the first eight provinces were each under a governor, while the latter six provinces were each under a chief commissioner. The viceroy directly governed the chief commissioner provinces through each respective chief commissioner, while the Presidencies and the provinces under governors were allowed greater autonomy under the Government of India Act.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinberg1947104–105,_108"India_–_Government_and_Constitution"-170) Each Presidency or province headed by a governor had either a provincial bicameral legislature (in the Presidencies, the United Provinces, Bihar and Assam) or a unicameral legislature (in the Punjab, Central Provinces and Berar, NWFP, Orissa and Sind). The governor of each presidency or province represented the Crown in his capacity, and was assisted by a ministers appointed from the members of each provincial legislature. Each provincial legislature had a life of five years, barring any special circumstances such as wartime conditions. All bills passed by the provincial legislature were either signed or rejected by the governor, who could also issue proclamations or promulgate ordinances while the legislature was in recess, as the need arose.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinberg1947108"India_–_Government_and_Constitution"-171)

Each province or presidency comprised a number of divisions, each headed by a [commissioner](/wiki/Divisional_commissioner_(India) "Divisional commissioner (India)") and subdivided into districts, which were the basic administrative units and each headed by a [district magistrate, collector or deputy commissioner](/wiki/District_collector_(India) "District collector (India)"); in 1947, British India comprised 230 districts.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinberg1947108"India_–_Government_and_Constitution"-171)

## Economy

Further information: [Economy of India under the British Raj](/wiki/Economy_of_India_under_the_British_Raj "Economy of India under the British Raj") and [Economic history of India](/wiki/Economic_history_of_India "Economic history of India")

### Economic trends

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/India_1862_One_Mohur.jpg/250px-India_1862_One_Mohur.jpg)](/wiki/File:India_1862_One_Mohur.jpg)

One [Mohur](/wiki/Mohur "Mohur") depicting [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") (1862)

All three sectors of the economy—agriculture, manufacturing, and services—accelerated in the postcolonial India. In agriculture a huge increase in production took place in the 1870s. The most important difference between colonial and postcolonial India was the use of land surplus with productivity-led growth by using high-yielding variety seeds, chemical fertilizers and more intensive application of water. All these three inputs were subsidised by the state.[[164]](#cite_note-172) The result was, on average, no long-term change in per capita income levels, though cost of living had grown higher. Agriculture was still dominant, with most peasants at the subsistence level. Extensive irrigation systems were built, providing an impetus for switching to cash crops for export and for raw materials for Indian industry, especially jute, cotton, sugarcane, coffee and tea.[[165]](#cite_note-173) India's global share of GDP fell drastically from above 20% to less than 5% in the colonial period.[[166]](#cite_note-174) Historians have been bitterly divided on issues of economic history, with the Nationalist school (following Nehru) arguing that India was poorer at the end of British rule than at the beginning and that impoverishment occurred because of the British.[[167]](#cite_note-175)

Mike Davis writes that much of the economic activity in British India was for the benefit of the British economy and was carried out relentlessly through repressive British imperial policies and with negative repercussions for the Indian population. This is reified in India's large exports of wheat to Britain: despite a major famine that claimed between 6 and 10 million lives in the late 1870s, these exports remained unchecked. A colonial government committed to laissez-faire economics refused to interfere with these exports or provide any relief.[[168]](#cite_note-176)

#### Industry

With the end of the state-granted monopoly of the [East India Trading Company](/wiki/East_India_Trading_Company "East India Trading Company") in 1813, the importation into India of British manufactured goods, including [finished textiles](/wiki/Textile_manufacturing "Textile manufacturing"), increased dramatically, from approximately 1 million yards of cotton cloth in 1814 to 13 million in 1820, 995 million in 1870, to 2050 million by 1890. The British imposed "[free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade")" on India, while continental Europe and the United States erected [stiff tariff](/wiki/Protectionism "Protectionism") barriers ranging from 30% to 70% on the importation of cotton yarn or prohibited it entirely. As a result of the [less expensive imports from more industrialized](/wiki/Infant_industry_argument "Infant industry argument") Britain, India's most significant industrial sector, [textile production](/wiki/Textile_industry_in_India "Textile industry in India"), [shrank](/wiki/Deindustrialization "Deindustrialization"), such that by 1870–1880 Indian producers were manufacturing only 25%–45% of local consumption. Deindustrialization of India's [iron industry](/wiki/Ferrous_metallurgy "Ferrous metallurgy") was even more extensive during this period.[[169]](#cite_note-177)

[Jamsetji Tata](/wiki/Jamsetji_Tata "Jamsetji Tata") (1839–1904) began his industrial career in 1877 with the Central India Spinning, Weaving, and Manufacturing Company in Bombay. While other Indian mills produced cheap coarse yarn (and later cloth) using local short-staple cotton and cheap machinery imported from Britain, Tata did much better by importing expensive longer-stapled cotton from Egypt and buying more complex ring-spindle machinery from the United States to spin finer yarn that could compete with imports from Britain.[[170]](#cite_note-dnb-178)

In the 1890s, he launched plans to move into heavy industry using Indian funding. The Raj did not provide capital, but, aware of Britain's declining position against the US and Germany in the steel industry, it wanted steel mills in India. It promised to purchase any surplus steel Tata could not otherwise sell.[[171]](#cite_note-179) The [Tata Iron and Steel Company](/wiki/Tata_Steel "Tata Steel") (TISCO), now headed by his son [Dorabji Tata](/wiki/Dorabji_Tata "Dorabji Tata") (1859–1932), began constructing its plant at Jamshedpur in Bihar in 1908, using American technology, not British.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeadrick1988291–92-180) According to *The Oxford Dictionary of National Biography*, TISCO became the leading iron and steel producer in India, and "a symbol of Indian technical skill, managerial competence, and entrepreneurial flair".[[170]](#cite_note-dnb-178) The Tata family, like most of India's big businessmen, were Indian nationalists but did not trust the Congress because it seemed too aggressively hostile to the Raj, too socialist, and too supportive of trade unions.[[173]](#cite_note-181)

#### Railways

Main article: [Rail transport in India § History](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_India#History "Rail transport in India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/IndianRailways1871b.jpg/250px-IndianRailways1871b.jpg)](/wiki/File:IndianRailways1871b.jpg)

The railway network of India in 1871, all major cities, Calcutta, Bombay and Madras, as well as Delhi, are connected.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/India_railways1909a.jpg/250px-India_railways1909a.jpg)](/wiki/File:India_railways1909a.jpg)

The railway network of India in 1909, when it was the fourth largest railway network in the world

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Victoriaterminus1903.JPG/250px-Victoriaterminus1903.JPG)](/wiki/File:Victoriaterminus1903.JPG)

"The most magnificent railway station in the world", says the caption of the stereographic tourist picture of [Victoria Terminus](/wiki/Victoria_Terminus "Victoria Terminus"), [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay"), which was completed in 1888.

British India built a modern railway system in the late 19th century, which was the fourth largest in the world. At first the railways were privately owned and operated. They were run by British administrators, engineers and craftsmen. At first, only the unskilled workers were Indians.[[174]](#cite_note-182)

The East India Company (and later the colonial government) encouraged new railway companies backed by private investors under a scheme that would provide land and guarantee an annual return of up to 5% during the initial years of operation. The companies were to build and operate the lines under a 99-year lease, with the government having the option to buy them earlier.[[175]](#cite_note-Bhandari_Chap1-2-183) Two new railway companies, the [Great Indian Peninsular Railway](/wiki/Great_Indian_Peninsular_Railway "Great Indian Peninsular Railway") (GIPR) and the [East Indian Railway Company](/wiki/East_Indian_Railway_Company "East Indian Railway Company") (EIR) began to construct and operate lines near Bombay and Calcutta in 1853–54. The first passenger railway line in North India, between Allahabad and Kanpur, opened in 1859. Eventually, five British companies came to own all railway business in India,[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESatya200872-184) and operated under a [profit maximization](/wiki/Profit_maximization "Profit maximization") scheme.[[177]](#cite_note-185) Further, there was no government regulation of these companies.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESatya200872-184)

In 1854, Governor-General [Lord Dalhousie](/wiki/Lord_Dalhousie "Lord Dalhousie") formulated a plan to construct a network of trunk lines connecting the principal regions of India. Encouraged by the government guarantees, investment flowed in and a series of new rail companies was established, leading to rapid expansion of the rail system in India.[[178]](#cite_note-Thorner-186) Soon several large princely states built their own rail systems and the network spread to the regions that became the modern-day states of [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam"), Rajasthan and [Andhra Pradesh](/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh"). The route mileage of this network increased from 1,349 to 25,495 kilometres (838 to 15,842 mi) between 1860 and 1890, mostly radiating inland from the three major port cities of Bombay, Madras, and Calcutta.[[179]](#cite_note-Hurd-187)

After the Sepoy Rebellion in 1857, and subsequent Crown rule over India, the railways were seen as a strategic defense of the European population, allowing the military to move quickly to subdue native unrest and protect Britons.[[180]](#cite_note-188) The railway thus served as a tool of the colonial government to control India as they were "an essential strategic, defensive, subjugators and administrative 'tool'" for the Imperial Project.[[181]](#cite_note-189)

Most of the railway construction was done by Indian companies supervised by British engineers.[[182]](#cite_note-Indian_railways-190) The system was heavily built, using a broad gauge, sturdy tracks and strong bridges. By 1900 India had a full range of rail services with diverse ownership and management, operating on broad, metre and [narrow gauge](/wiki/Narrow_gauge "Narrow gauge") networks. In 1900, the government took over the GIPR network, while the company continued to manage it.[[182]](#cite_note-Indian_railways-190) During the First World War, the railways were used to transport troops and grain to the ports of Bombay and Karachi en route to Britain, [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), and [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] With shipments of equipment and parts from Britain curtailed, maintenance became much more difficult; critical workers entered the army; workshops were converted to making munitions; the locomotives, rolling stock, and track of some entire lines were shipped to the Middle East. The railways could barely keep up with the increased demand.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeadrick198878–79-191) By the end of the war, the railways had deteriorated for lack of maintenance and were not profitable. In 1923, both GIPR and EIR were nationalised.[[184]](#cite_note-192)[[185]](#cite_note-h1-193)

Headrick shows that until the 1930s, both the Raj lines and the private companies hired only European supervisors, civil engineers, and even operating personnel, such as locomotive engineers. The hard physical labor was left to the Indians. The colonial government was chiefly concerned with the welfare of European workers, and any Indian deaths were "either ignored or merely mentioned as a cold statistical figure."[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESatya200873-194)[[187]](#cite_note-195) The government's Stores Policy required that bids on railway contracts be made to the India Office in London, shutting out most Indian firms.[[185]](#cite_note-h1-193) The railway companies purchased most of their hardware and parts in Britain. There were railway maintenance workshops in India, but they were rarely allowed to manufacture or repair locomotives.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeadrick198881–82-196)

After independence in 1947, forty-two separate railway systems, including thirty-two lines owned by the former Indian princely states, were amalgamated to form a single nationalised unit named the *Indian Railways*.

India provides an example of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") pouring its money and expertise into a very well-built system designed for military purposes (after the Rebellion of 1857), in the hope that it would stimulate industry. The system was overbuilt and too expensive for the small amount of freight traffic it carried. Christensen (1996), who looked at colonial purpose, local needs, capital, service, and private-versus-public interests, concluded that making the railways a creature of the state hindered success because railway expenses had to go through the same time-consuming and political budgeting process as did all other state expenses. Railway costs could therefore not be tailored to the current needs of the railways or of their passengers.[[189]](#cite_note-197)

#### Irrigation

Main article: [Irrigation in India](/wiki/Irrigation_in_India "Irrigation in India")

The British Raj invested heavily in infrastructure, including canals and irrigation systems.[[190]](#cite_note-198) The Ganges Canal reached 560 kilometres (350 miles) from Haridwar to Cawnpore (now Kanpur), and supplied thousands of kilometres of distribution canals. By 1900 the Raj had the largest irrigation system in the world. One success story was Assam, a jungle in 1840 that by 1900 had 1,600,000 hectares (4,000,000 acres) under cultivation, especially in tea plantations. In all, the amount of irrigated land rose eightfold. Historian [David Gilmour](/wiki/Sir_David_Gilmour,_4th_Baronet "Sir David Gilmour, 4th Baronet") says:[[191]](#cite_note-199)

> By the 1870s the peasantry in the districts irrigated by the Ganges Canal were visibly better fed, housed and dressed than before; by the end of the century the new network of canals in the Punjab had produced an even more prosperous peasantry there.

### Economic impact of the Raj

Historians continue to debate whether the long-term intention of British rule was to accelerate the economic development of India, or to distort and delay it. In 1780, the conservative British politician [Edmund Burke](/wiki/Edmund_Burke "Edmund Burke") raised the issue of India's position: he vehemently attacked the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company"), claiming that [Warren Hastings](/wiki/Warren_Hastings "Warren Hastings") and other top officials had ruined the Indian economy and society. Indian historian Rajat Kanta Ray (1998) continues this line of attack, saying the new economy brought by the British in the 18th century was a form of "plunder" and a catastrophe for the traditional economy of the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire").[[192]](#cite_note-British_expansion_in_India-200) Ray accuses the British of depleting the food and money stocks and of imposing high taxes that helped cause the terrible [Bengal famine of 1770](/wiki/Bengal_famine_of_1770 "Bengal famine of 1770"), which killed a third of the people of Bengal.[[193]](#cite_note-201)

[P. J. Marshall](/wiki/P._J._Marshall "P. J. Marshall") shows that recent scholarship has reinterpreted the view that the prosperity of the formerly benign Mughal rule gave way to poverty and anarchy.[[194]](#cite_note-Impact_of_British_Rule-202) He argues the British takeover did not make any sharp break with the past, which largely delegated control to regional Mughal rulers and sustained a generally prosperous economy for the rest of the 18th century. Marshall notes the British went into partnership with Indian bankers and raised revenue through local tax administrators and kept the old Mughal rates of taxation.

The East India Company inherited an onerous taxation system that took one-third of the produce of Indian cultivators.[[192]](#cite_note-British_expansion_in_India-200) Instead of the Indian nationalist account of the British as alien aggressors, seizing power by brute force and impoverishing all of India, Marshall presents the interpretation (supported by many scholars in India and the West) that the British were not in full control but instead were players in what was primarily an Indian play and in which their rise to power depended upon excellent co-operation with Indian elites.[[194]](#cite_note-Impact_of_British_Rule-202) Marshall admits that much of his interpretation is still highly controversial among many historians.[[195]](#cite_note-203)

## Demography

Main articles: [Census of British India](/wiki/Census_of_British_India "Census of British India"), [Demographics of India](/wiki/Demographics_of_India "Demographics of India"), [Demographics of Myanmar](/wiki/Demographics_of_Myanmar "Demographics of Myanmar"), [Demographics of Pakistan](/wiki/Demographics_of_Pakistan "Demographics of Pakistan"), and [Demographics of Bangladesh](/wiki/Demographics_of_Bangladesh "Demographics of Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Census_of_British_India_by_religion%2C_1921.jpg/250px-Census_of_British_India_by_religion%2C_1921.jpg)](/wiki/File:Census_of_British_India_by_religion,_1921.jpg)

The [1921 census of British India](/wiki/Census_in_British_India "Census in British India") shows 69 million Muslims and 217 million Hindus out of a total population of 316 million.

The population of the territory that became the British Raj was 100 million by 1600 and remained nearly stationary until the 19th century. The population of the Raj reached 255 million according to the first census taken in 1881 of India.[[196]](#cite_note-204)[[197]](#cite_note-205)[[198]](#cite_note-206)[[199]](#cite_note-207)

Studies of India's population since 1881 have focused on such topics as total population, birth and death rates, growth rates, geographic distribution, literacy, the rural and urban divide, cities of a million, and the three cities with populations over eight million: [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), Greater [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay"), and [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta").[[200]](#cite_note-208)

Mortality rates fell in the 1920–1945 era, primarily due to biological immunisation. Other factors included rising incomes and better living conditions, improved nutrition, a safer and cleaner environment, and better official health policies and medical care.[[201]](#cite_note-209)

Severe overcrowding in the cities caused major public health problems, as noted in an official report from 1938:[[202]](#cite_note-210)

:   In the urban and industrial areas ... cramped sites, the high values of land and the necessity for the worker to live in the vicinity of his work ... all tend to intensify congestion and overcrowding. In the busiest centres houses are built close together, eave touching eave, and frequently back to back .... Space is so valuable that, in place of streets and roads, winding lanes provide the only approach to the houses. Neglect of sanitation is often evidenced by heaps of rotting garbage and pools of sewage, whilst the absence of latrines enhance the general pollution of air and soil.

## Famines, epidemics, and public health

Main articles: [Famine in India § British rule](/wiki/Famine_in_India#British_rule "Famine in India"), and [Timeline of major famines in India during British rule](/wiki/Timeline_of_major_famines_in_India_during_British_rule "Timeline of major famines in India during British rule")

During the British Raj, India experienced [a large number of major famines](/wiki/Famine_in_India "Famine in India"), including the [Great Famine of 1876–1878](/wiki/Great_Famine_of_1876%E2%80%931878 "Great Famine of 1876–1878"), in which 6.1 million to 10.39 million Indians perished[[203]](#cite_note-mikedavis-211) and the [Indian famine of 1899–1900](/wiki/Indian_famine_of_1899%E2%80%931900 "Indian famine of 1899–1900"), in which 1.25 to 10 million Indians perished.[[204]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-212)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Bengal_famine_1943_photo.jpg/250px-Bengal_famine_1943_photo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bengal_famine_1943_photo.jpg)

Child who starved to death during the [Bengal famine of 1943](/wiki/Bengal_famine_of_1943 "Bengal famine of 1943")

The [first cholera pandemic](/wiki/1817%E2%80%931824_cholera_pandemic "1817–1824 cholera pandemic") began in [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), then spread across India by 1820. Ten thousand British troops and countless Indians died during this [pandemic](/wiki/Pandemic "Pandemic").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Estimated deaths in India between 1817 and 1860 exceeded 15 million. Another 23 million died between 1865 and 1917.[[205]](#cite_note-213) The [Third plague pandemic](/wiki/Third_plague_pandemic "Third plague pandemic") which started in China in the middle of the 19th century, eventually spread to all inhabited continents and killed 10 million Indians in India alone.[[206]](#cite_note-214) [Waldemar Haffkine](/wiki/Waldemar_Haffkine "Waldemar Haffkine"), who mainly worked in India, became the first [microbiologist](/wiki/Microbiologist "Microbiologist") to develop and deploy [vaccines](/wiki/Vaccine "Vaccine") against cholera and bubonic plague. In 1925 the Plague Laboratory in Bombay was renamed the [Haffkine Institute](/wiki/Haffkine_Institute "Haffkine Institute").

Fevers ranked as one of the leading causes of death in India in the 19th century.[[207]](#cite_note-215) Britain's [Sir Ronald Ross](/wiki/Ronald_Ross "Ronald Ross"), working in the [Presidency General Hospital](/wiki/Presidency_General_Hospital "Presidency General Hospital") in [Calcutta](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata"), finally proved in 1898 that mosquitoes transmit [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria"), while on assignment in the Deccan at Secunderabad, where the Centre for Tropical and Communicable Diseases is now named in his honour.[[208]](#cite_note-216)

In 1881 there were around 120,000 [leprosy](/wiki/Leprosy "Leprosy") patients. The central government passed the [Lepers Act of 1898](/wiki/List_of_Indian_Federal_Legislation "List of Indian Federal Legislation"), which provided legal provision for forcible confinement of people with leprosy in India.[[209]](#cite_note-217) Under the direction of [Mountstuart Elphinstone](/wiki/Mountstuart_Elphinstone "Mountstuart Elphinstone") a program was launched to propagate [smallpox vaccination](/wiki/Smallpox_vaccine "Smallpox vaccine").[[210]](#cite_note-218) Mass vaccination in India resulted in a major decline in smallpox mortality by the end of the 19th century.[[211]](#cite_note-219) In 1849 nearly 13% of all Calcutta deaths were due to [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox").[[212]](#cite_note-220) Between 1868 and 1907, there were approximately 4.7 million deaths from smallpox.[[213]](#cite_note-221)

Sir [Robert Grant](/wiki/Robert_Grant_(MP) "Robert Grant (MP)") directed his attention to establishing a systematic institution in Bombay for imparting medical knowledge to the natives.[[214]](#cite_note-222) In 1860, [Grant Medical College](/wiki/Grant_Medical_College "Grant Medical College") became one of the four recognised colleges for teaching courses leading to degrees (alongside [Elphinstone College](/wiki/Elphinstone_College "Elphinstone College"), [Deccan College](/wiki/Deccan_College_(Pune) "Deccan College (Pune)") and [Government Law College, Mumbai](/wiki/Government_Law_College,_Mumbai "Government Law College, Mumbai")).[[194]](#cite_note-Impact_of_British_Rule-202)

## Education

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Lucknowuniversity.jpg/250px-Lucknowuniversity.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lucknowuniversity.jpg)

The [University of Lucknow](/wiki/University_of_Lucknow "University of Lucknow"), founded by the British in 1867

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Calcuttamedicalcollege1.jpg/330px-Calcuttamedicalcollege1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calcuttamedicalcollege1.jpg)

The [University of Calcutta](/wiki/University_of_Calcutta "University of Calcutta"), established in 1857, is one of the three oldest modern [state universities](/wiki/State_university_(India) "State university (India)") in India.

Universities in Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras were established in 1857, just before the Rebellion. By 1890 some 60,000 Indians had matriculated, chiefly in the [liberal arts](/wiki/Liberal_arts_education "Liberal arts education") or law. About a third entered public administration, and another third became lawyers. The result was a very well educated professional state bureaucracy. By 1887 of 21,000 mid-level civil services appointments, 45% were held by Hindus, 7% by Muslims, 19% by Eurasians (European father and Indian mother), and 29% by Europeans. Of the 1000 top-level civil services positions, almost all were held by Britons, typically with an [Oxbridge](/wiki/Oxbridge "Oxbridge") degree.[[215]](#cite_note-223) The government, often working with local philanthropists, opened 186 universities and colleges of higher education by 1911; they enrolled 36,000 students (over 90% men). By 1939 the number of institutions had doubled and enrolment reached 145,000. The curriculum followed classical British standards of the sort set by Oxford and Cambridge and stressed English literature and European history. Nevertheless, by the 1920s the student bodies had become hotbeds of Indian nationalism.[[216]](#cite_note-224)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** a quasi-federation of [presidencies and provinces](/wiki/Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India "Presidencies and provinces of British India") directly governed by [the British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") through the [Viceroy and Governor-General of India](/wiki/Viceroy_and_Governor-General_of_India "Viceroy and Governor-General of India")
2. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** governed by Indian rulers, under the [suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty "Suzerainty") of The British Crown exercised through the Viceroy of India)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** [Simla](/wiki/Simla "Simla") was the [summer capital](/wiki/Summer_capital "Summer capital") of the Government of [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India"), not of the British Raj, i.e. the British Indian Empire, which included the [Princely States](/wiki/Princely_State "Princely State").[[4]](#cite_note-simla-summer-capital-6)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** The proclamation for New Delhi to be the capital was made in 1911, but the city was inaugurated as the capital of the Raj in February 1931.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** English was the language of the courts and government.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** Urdu, along with Hindi, was also given official status in large parts of northern India, as were vernaculars elsewhere.[[5]](#cite_note-lelyveld-hindustani-10)[[6]](#cite_note-dalby-andrew-hindi-11)[[7]](#cite_note-hindi-12)[[8]](#cite_note-everaert-urdu-official-13)[[9]](#cite_note-dhir-urdu-14)[[10]](#cite_note-bayly-bentinck-era-15)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-78)** Seated l. to r. are: Jiddhu Krisnamurthi, Besant, and [Charles Webster Leadbeater](/wiki/Charles_Webster_Leadbeater "Charles Webster Leadbeater").
8. **[^](#cite_ref-138)** The only other emperor during this period, [Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII") (reigned January to December 1936), did not issue any Indian currency under his name.

5. **[^](#cite_ref-lelyveld-hindustani_10-0)** Lelyveld, David (1993). ["Colonial Knowledge and the Fate of Hindustani"](https://www.jstor.org/stable/179178). *Comparative Studies in Society and History*. **35** (4): 665–682. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1017/S0010417500018661](https://doi.org/10.1017%2FS0010417500018661). [ISSN](/wiki/ISSN_(identifier) "ISSN (identifier)") [0010-4175](https://search.worldcat.org/issn/0010-4175). [JSTOR](/wiki/JSTOR_(identifier) "JSTOR (identifier)") [179178](https://www.jstor.org/stable/179178). [S2CID](/wiki/S2CID_(identifier) "S2CID (identifier)") [144180838](https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusID:144180838). [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20230408220233/https://www.jstor.org/stable/179178) from the original on 8 April 2023. Retrieved 8 April 2023. "The earlier grammars and dictionaries made it possible for the British government to replace Persian with vernacular languages at the lower levels of the judicial and revenue administration in 1837, that is, to standardize and index terminology for official use and provide for its translation to the language of the ultimate ruling authority, English. For such purposes, Hindustani was equated with Urdu, as opposed to any geographically defined dialect of Hindi and was given official status through large parts of north India. Written in the Persian script with a largely Persian and, via Persian, an Arabic vocabulary, Urdu stood at the shortest distance from the previous situation and was easily attainable by the same personnel. In the wake of this official transformation, the British government began to make its first significant efforts on behalf of vernacular education."
6. **[^](#cite_ref-dalby-andrew-hindi_11-0)** Dalby, Andrew (2004) [1998]. "Hindi". *A Dictionary of Languages: The definitive reference to more than 400 languages*. A & C Black Publishers. p. 248. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-7136-7841-3](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-7136-7841-3 "Special:BookSources/978-0-7136-7841-3"). "In the government of northern India Persian ruled. Under the British Raj, Persian eventually declined, but, the administration remaining largely Muslim, the role of Persian was taken not by Hindi but by Urdu, known to the British as Hindustani. It was only as the Hindu majority in India began to assert itself that Hindi came into its own."
7. **[^](#cite_ref-hindi_12-0)** Vejdani, Farzin (2015), *Making History in Iran: Education, Nationalism, and Print Culture*, Stanford, CA: Stanford University Press, pp. 24–25, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-8047-9153-3](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-8047-9153-3 "Special:BookSources/978-0-8047-9153-3"), "Although the official languages of administration in India shifted from Persian to English and Urdu in 1837, Persian continued to be taught and read there through the early twentieth century."

9. **[^](#cite_ref-dhir-urdu_14-0)** Dhir, Krishna S. (2022). *The Wonder That Is Urdu*. Delhi: Motilal Banarsidass. p. 138. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-81-208-4301-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-81-208-4301-1 "Special:BookSources/978-81-208-4301-1"). "The British used the Urdu language to effect a shift from the prior emphasis on the Persian language. In 1837, the British East India Company adopted Urdu in place of Persian as the co-official language in India, along with English. In the law courts in Bengal and the North-West Provinces and Oudh (modern day Uttar Pradesh) a highly technical form of Urdu was used in the Nastaliq script, by both Muslims and Hindus. The same was the case in the government offices. In the various other regions of India, local vernaculars were used as official language in the lower courts and in government offices. ... In certain parts South Asia, Urdu was written in several scripts. Kaithi was a popular script used for both Urdu and Hindi. By 1880, Kaithi was used as court language in Bihar. However, in 1881, Hindi in Devanagari script replaced Urdu in the Nastaliq script in Bihar. In Panjab, Urdu was written in Nastaliq, Devanagari, Kaithi, and Gurumukhi.  
    In April 1900, the colonial government of the North-West Provinces and Oudh granted equal official status to both, Devanagari and Nastaliq scripts. However, Nastaliq remained the dominant script. During the 1920s, Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi deplored the controversy and the evolving divergence between Urdu and Hindi, exhorting the remerging of the two languages as Hindustani. However, Urdu continued to draw from Persian, Arabic, and Chagtai, while Hindi did the same from Sanskrit. Eventually, the controversy resulted in the loss of the official status of the Urdu language."
10. **[^](#cite_ref-bayly-bentinck-era_15-0)** Bayly, C. A. (1988). *Indian Society and the making of the British Empire*. New Cambridge History of India series. Cambridge University Press. p. 122. "The use of Persian was abolished in official correspondence (1835); the government's weight was thrown behind English-medium education and Thomas Babington Macaulay's Codes of Criminal and Civil Procedure (drafted 1841–2, but not completed until the 1860s) sought to impose a rational, Western legal system on the amalgam of Muslim, Hindu and English law which had been haphazardly administered in British courts. The fruits of the Bentinck era were significant. But they were only of general importance in so far as they went with the grain of social changes which were already gathering pace in India. The Bombay and Calcutta intelligentsia were taking to English education well before the Education Minute of 1836. Flowery Persian was already giving way in north India to the fluid and demotic Urdu. As for changes in the legal system, they were only implemented after the Rebellion of 1857 when communications improved and more substantial sums of money were made available for education."

(The British) expanded their influence, winning political control of Bengal and Bihar after the Battle of Plassey in 1757.
